Historical hash algorithms must remain unchanged for existing evidence.

Proposed fix

Introduce audit schema v5 and encode Option values injectively: hash a presence discriminator byte, then the value bytes only for Some. Audit all other optional/enumerated fields for ambiguous sentinel/concatenation encodings and define one canonical, length-delimited hash serialization. Preserve v1-v4 verification exactly; new records use v5 and updated signatures.

Guard tests

1. For v5, changing None to Some(u64::MAX), Some(0), or any value changes entry_hash and invalidates MAC/verify.
2. None, Some(0), Some(u64::MAX), and ordinary counts round-trip distinctly.
3. Property test over Option<u64> proves unequal semantic values produce unequal canonical encodings before hashing.
4. Mutation suite flips presence/value for every optional field and expects verification failure.
5. Golden v1-v4 chains continue to verify byte-for-byte; v5 mixed/rotated chains verify with correct schema dispatch.
6. CEF/syslog/JSON consumers handle the boundary value without interpreting it as absent.
close_reason: Confirmed semantic collision across v1-v4. Added audit schema v5 with injective length-framed canonical encoding, explicit Option tags and stable enum tags; preserved exact v1-v4 verification; added tamper, property, mixed-chain, all-field and shipping boundary tests. Audit 102/102 green.

title: [QA100][P2][telemetry] Deny sensitive db.* attributes before safe-key handling
description:
Finding

The OTLP redactor treats every db.* attribute as known-safe before consulting the broad sensitive-key denylist. Consequently db.password, db.token, db.authorization, db.connection_string, db.wallet_secret, and similar names pass through with their values verbatim, directly contradicting the redactor contract. Current in-repo emitters were not found using those names, so this is a latent/defense-in-depth leak rather than a proven v0.8.0 emission.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-telemetry/src/otlp/redact.rs:39-59 special-cases only db.statement/db.query.text/parameter, then returns false from should_drop_key for is_known_safe_key before checking SENSITIVE_KEY_FRAGMENTS.
- is_known_safe_key at lines 124-130 returns true for every db. prefix.
- redact_value at lines 63-75 likewise returns every known-safe key value unchanged before value_looks_secret, so even Bearer-shaped values survive.
- The module contract at lines 1-16 says password/token/wallet/bind keys are dropped and every exported attribute passes this funnel. OTLP logs/spans accept tracing fields from production dependencies, not only a closed internal enum.
- Independent direct calls proved db.password, db.token with Bearer content, db.connection_string, and db.wallet_secret all produce drop=false and unchanged values.
- Fresh source search found current driver/application emitters using safe db fields and db.statement covered by the special deny, but no presently sensitive db.* callsite. That limits current exploitability without making the redactor implementation correct.
- A full unlimited tracker search found generic redaction work but no prefix-order bug.

Impact

A future application/dependency span or log following an intuitive db.password/token/connection_string name silently exports the credential to the configured OTLP collector. Because this component is advertised as the universal secret boundary, callers may reasonably rely on it rather than duplicate filtering.

False-positive checks

OTel defines many legitimate db.* semantic keys, but it does not make arbitrary prefixed extensions non-secret. The correct safe set is a finite exact allowlist. This bead is intentionally P2 hardening because no current sensitive emitter was found; a live emitting callsite would raise severity.

Proposed fix

Evaluate sensitive key fragments before any general allowlist, retaining explicit raw-SQL denies. Replace blanket db.* trust with exact safe OTel database semantic keys the product emits/needs, and apply value-shape redaction even to structured keys unless their type/domain makes secrets impossible. Centralize the policy for logs, spans, and metrics.

Guard tests

1. Cartesian/case variants of db.password, passwd, secret, token, authorization, credential, wallet, bind, dsn, connect_string, and connection_string are dropped.
2. Bearer/opaque/key=value secret-shaped values under unknown and extension keys are redacted.
3. Exact benign keys such as db.system.name, db.namespace, db.operation.name, response status, and the hash-only approved field retain expected values.
4. OTLP protobuf log/span wire snapshots contain no sentinel from sensitive db.* fields.
5. Dependency-emitted tracing events pass the same policy; a lint/test prevents adding new wildcard safe prefixes.
6. Existing db.statement/query-text/parameter denial remains fail-closed.
close_reason: Confirmed arbitrary sensitive db.* attributes bypassed both key and value redaction. Replaced wildcard trust with finite exact db keys, typed numeric bind counters, universal secret-shape scanning, and final trace protobuf re-filtering; added direct/log/metric/dependency/raw-wire sentinel tests. Telemetry 45/45 and all-target clippy green.

title: [QA100][P1][SIEM] Prevent RFC5424 line injection from audit messages
description:
Finding

The RFC-5424 formatter escapes structured-data fields but interpolates sql_preview raw into the MSG portion. Valid multiline SQL comments can include CR/LF and a forged PRI/header, causing one signed audit record to become multiple physical syslog lines and letting downstream line-oriented collectors interpret attacker-controlled text as a separate event.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/shipping.rs:301-328 constructs syslog structured data with push_sd_param, then formats record.sql_preview directly as the trailing message.
- push_sd_param at lines 401-417 replaces CR/LF and escapes RFC structured-data delimiters, proving the code recognizes that boundary, but no equivalent MSG sanitizer exists.
- AuditRecord currently copies the first 120 SQL characters verbatim, and oracle_execute accepts multiline/commented SQL through its normal classifier.
- An independent current-rlib proof used valid-shaped UPDATE SQL with a multiline comment whose second line began a synthetic <PRI>1 header; syslog_line returned two physical lines and the second began with the forged header. CEF correctly escaped newlines.
- Existing syslog tests assert header/structured fields and severity, not exactly-one-line/control-character invariants. A full unlimited tracker scan found no syslog injection issue.

Impact

An authenticated SQL caller can forge visual/event boundaries in syslog-native SIEM pipelines, corrupt RFC-5424 framing, create false alerts, or obscure the relationship between signed structured data and displayed message. The injected second line is not independently signed even though a collector may treat it as an event.

False-positive checks

RFC-5424 MSG can carry UTF-8, but line-oriented transports/collectors require framing discipline and C0 controls remain dangerous. The SQL itself need not bypass the classifier; an ordinary comment supplies the bytes. QA100 .31 removes raw literals/previews at the source, but a wire formatter must still enforce its own one-event invariant for historical/imported records and any future message field.

Proposed fix

Render a hash-only or literal-free summary in MSG and sanitize all remaining message text to exactly one RFC-compliant record: fold/escape CR, LF, NUL, TAB as policy dictates, reject/encode other C0 and DEL, and preserve valid Unicode. Prefer octet-counted framing where supported while still producing a safe standalone line. Keep signed chain fields in structured data.

Guard tests

1. CR, LF, CRLF, NUL, TAB, ESC, DEL, and forged PRI/header sequences produce exactly one physical syslog record with no prohibited controls.
2. Multiline ordinary/N/q-quoted SQL and comments cannot create another event boundary.
3. Valid Unicode survives according to RFC policy.
4. Structured-data escaping, severity, hashes, key id, and signature remain intact.
5. Collector-level fixture parses one input record into exactly one event.
6. QA100 .31 sentinel literals remain absent from syslog regardless of formatter sanitization.
close_reason: Confirmed with a genuinely signed v1 record: raw sql_preview CRLF produced a forged second RFC5424 line. syslog MSG now omits historical/current SQL text, emits a control-encoded literal-free tool/hash summary, hardens SD controls, preserves Unicode and chain fields, and has signed-legacy/dialect/collector regression coverage. Audit fmt, clippy -D warnings, 108 tests, and core forwarder tests pass.

title: [QA100][P2][pool] Use RAII so dropped futures cannot leak pool capacity
description:
Finding

OraclePool accounting cleanup exists only in code that runs after awaited operations return. If the checkout/query future is dropped while Pending, Rust drops the physical connection/future but never decrements reserved open_count or checked-out in_use/acquired state. Repeated task abort/runtime teardown can permanently exhaust a live pool even though no connections remain usable.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/pool.rs:342-363 obtains a connection, increments in_use/acquired, awaits the caller future and health check, then checks in. Its comment calls cleanup unconditional, but async Future::drop skips every statement after the suspension point.
- try_checkout at lines 391-407 removes an idle connection before awaiting is_valid; drop during validation loses the connection without forget_open_connection.
- The same function reserves open_count before awaiting manager.connect; drop during connect leaves the reservation.
- Drop during f(cx, conn).await or manager.has_broken.await leaves in_use/open/acquired unbalanced while the owned connection is dropped.
- Existing cancelled_call_discards_checked_out_connection tests an already-returned Err(Cancelled), which executes cleanup; it does not poll a future to Pending then drop it. Accounting tests call record_checkin directly.
- Production constructs OraclePool for profiles.pool and routes metadata/query/describe/ping operations through with_conn. Normal cooperative Cx cancellation often returns Err and is handled; abrupt task/future drop remains reachable during abort, panic unwinding, runtime shutdown, or consumer cancellation.
- A full unlimited tracker scan found poison/quarantine work but no future-drop reservation leak.

Impact

After enough dropped operations, open_count reaches max_size with no idle or truly in-use connections. All later checkouts spin until BUSY timeout and the pool cannot self-heal without restart. Metrics falsely report leaked in-use/acquired capacity and the advertised zero-leak invariant fails.

False-positive checks

Rust does not run the remainder of an async fn on Future::drop; only destructors of fields/state run. Cooperative cancellation that resolves to an error is covered but cannot substitute for cancellation safety at every suspension point. This is P2 reliability because abrupt drop is less common than ordinary Cx cancellation, not because the accounting is correct.

Proposed fix

Represent each reservation/checkout as an RAII guard holding pool state and optionally the connection. Guard Drop must release reserved/open/in-use capacity and treat an abruptly dropped checked-out connection as dirty/discarded; explicit successful transfer/checkin disarms or converts the guard. Avoid blocking I/O in Drop. Apply the guard before every await that follows removal/reservation.

Guard tests

1. With a fake manager/future that stays Pending, poll then drop during new-connect reservation; open capacity returns and a replacement checkout succeeds.
2. Repeat during idle validation, caller query body, and post-success health ping.
3. After each drop, open/in_use/acquired == released+discarded according to documented accounting and no dirty connection is reused.
4. Panic unwinding and runtime/task cancellation exercise the same RAII paths.
5. Cooperative success/error/cancel behavior remains unchanged; dirty outcomes discard and clean outcomes return idle.
6. Stress/model test repeatedly drops at every suspension point and proves max_size capacity never leaks.
close_reason: Already fixed in main (verified at ce4c6a7): pool.rs installs RAII drop-guards PendingOpenSlot (Drop releases reserved open_count) before both is_valid().await and connect().await in try_checkout, and CheckedOutConnection<T> (Drop does synchronous dirty-discard via record_checkin) before f().await and has_broken().await in with_conn. A dropped/cancelled future can no longer leak in_use/open_count. Committed as part of the pool-hardening range (qa30/qa48/qa85). A parallel worktree agent independently reproduced the identical fix on a stale base (7b196c6) — duplicate discarded, not integrated.

title: [QA100][P1][audit] Implement usable mixed-key audit rotation and verification
description:
Finding

The audit format records key_id for rotation, and docs instruct replacing key_ref/key_id then restarting while retaining old keys, but configuration and CLI can supply only one key. A normal key change makes startup reject the existing old-key anchor; if the anchor is manually reset, mixed-key history can no longer be verified or restored with the one-key CLI. The documented rotation procedure is not implementable.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-config/src/lib.rs:84-150 exposes one AuditConfig.key_ref and one key_id, with no historical verification-key collection.
- Auditor::verify_anchor_authenticity at crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/sink.rs:583-604 requires the existing anchor key_id to equal the new active signing key and verifies only with that key. After key1 -> key2 config change, restart fails closed before a key2 record can be appended.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:3903-3929 audit_verification_keys constructs exactly one SigningKey. --key-id only relabels the same resolved current secret; it does not add an old secret/key pair.
- verify_records correctly requires a matching key for every record key_id, so a mixed key1/key2 chain returns UnknownKeyId with either single-key invocation. Restore uses the same one-key vector.
- docs/operations.md:832-840 says to add a new key/id, restart, and keep old ids available; ADR 0003 says historical records remain verifiable. No syntax or store exists to do so.
- The prior forged-anchor fix intentionally rejects unknown key ids, which is correct fail-closed behavior but exposes the missing explicit rotation protocol.
- A full unlimited tracker search found the anchor fix and original keyed-chain feature, but no rotation/keyring implementation bug.

Impact

Operators cannot rotate a compromised/aged audit signing key without either making the service fail to start or manually weakening/removing anchor continuity and losing end-to-end verification/restore of the mixed chain. This defeats an advertised security lifecycle and encourages unsafe recovery steps.

False-positive checks

Failing closed on an unknown anchor key is correct; silently trusting or resetting it would recreate a truncation bypass. The missing piece is authenticated access to both old and new keys plus an explicit transition. Rotating the key behind the same key_id is even less distinguishable and must be rejected or versioned explicitly.

Proposed fix

Add a redacted audit keyring configuration/CLI surface: exactly one active signing key and zero or more historical verification keys keyed by unique id. During an explicit rotation, authenticate the old anchor with its historical key, verify the full chain, seed continuation, and atomically advance the anchor under the new active key only after the first new durable record. CLI verify, startup, backup, and restore consume the same keyring. Never log/render key refs or values.

Guard tests

1. key1 records+anchor -> configure key1 historical and key2 active -> restart succeeds -> key2 append -> full mixed chain and anchor verify with both.
2. CLI audit verify and service restore accept the keyring and identify missing/wrong old keys fail closed.
3. Duplicate key ids, same id with changed bytes, no active key for writable profiles, and ambiguous rotations are rejected.
4. Crash before/after first key2 record yields a recoverable old-anchor or new-anchor state, never an unauthenticated gap.
5. Key material and reference locators remain absent from Debug, errors, audit, telemetry, and protocol.
6. v1-v4/v5 schema verification and WORM/SIEM streams preserve mixed-key order and signatures.
close_reason: Confirmed normal key1-to-key2 rotation failed startup on the authenticated key1 anchor and one-key CLI/restore could not verify mixed history. Added one active signer plus unique historical verification keyring across config, startup, CLI, backup, and restore; full-chain/anchor authentication; unique safe IDs/material; no key epoch reuse; redacted resolution; atomic old-to-new anchor transition only after the first active durable record; crash-window recovery; and mixed-version, WORM, SIEM, CLI, and restore guards.

title: [QA100][P1][supply-chain] Pin and verify privileged workflow dependencies
description:
Finding

Release and registry jobs execute mutable, unverified third-party code while holding publication authority. The MCP registry jobs download releases/latest through curl into tar and execute mcp-publisher with id-token: write. Workflows also use mutable action tags or branches, including dtolnay/rust-toolchain@master and version tags for release/build/signing actions.

Evidence

- .github/workflows/release.yml:655-675 grants OIDC, fetches mcp-publisher from releases/latest with curl -L piped directly to tar, then logs in and publishes.
- .github/workflows/publish-mcp.yml:18-40 repeats the pattern in a dispatch job with OIDC; curl lacks --fail and the archive has no pinned version, digest, signature, or provenance check.
- Repository workflows use mutable third-party refs such as dtolnay/rust-toolchain@master, softprops/action-gh-release@v3, taiki-e/install-action@v2 and version tags for Docker/signing actions rather than immutable commit SHAs.

Impact

A moved upstream release asset/tag, compromised upstream account, or tag retarget can run attacker-controlled code with registry OIDC, package, attestation, or release permissions. Piping an unchecked HTTP error/body to tar also produces opaque failures. This is a privileged supply-chain boundary, not a generic dependabot preference.

False-positive and duplicate checks

GitHub resolves tags normally, but tags and releases/latest are mutable identities. The jobs explicitly grant outward-facing authority and execute fetched code before publication. A full repo-local tracker search found no action-pinning or mcp-publisher verification bead.

Proposed fix

Pin every third-party action to a reviewed full commit SHA with a human-readable version comment. Pin a specific mcp-publisher version and platform artifact; download with curl --fail --location --proto =https --tlsv1.2 to a file, verify a repository-pinned SHA-256 or signed provenance/attestation, then extract. Keep OIDC/package permissions at the smallest publishing step/job and do validation/build without them. Add an explicit update process (Dependabot/Renovate or reviewed script) for pins.

Guard tests

- Repository lint rejects non-SHA third-party uses refs while allowing local actions and approved first-party exceptions.
- Lint rejects executable downloads from releases/latest and curl-to-shell/tar in privileged jobs.
- Fixture with wrong publisher digest fails before extraction/login; correct fixture succeeds.
- OIDC and package write permissions are absent from build/test jobs and present only where consumed.
- Release/registry dry-run proves the pinned publisher platform matrix is complete.
close_reason: Verified privileged supply-chain gap. Pinned all non-GitHub remote actions to reviewed commit SHAs; replaced mutable releases/latest curl-to-tar publisher install with v1.7.9 plus repository-pinned six-platform SHA-256 verification before extraction; narrowed MCP OIDC jobs; added permission/action/download guards and Dependabot updates. Guards passed bash -n, shellcheck, actionlint, workflow policy negative checksum/order checks, real verified publisher execution, pinned upstream checksum comparison, Docker provenance validator, and diff checks.

title: [QA100][P1][release-security] Treat npm dispatch inputs as data and bind publish to its tag
description:
Finding

The manual npm publication workflow interpolates free-form workflow_dispatch inputs directly into Bash source and lets checkout_ref select arbitrary repository code for an immutable package publication.

Evidence

- .github/workflows/publish-npm.yml:3-22 declares version and checkout_ref as unconstrained strings.
- Lines 38-41 check out checkout_ref when supplied rather than the release tag derived from version.
- Lines 50-55 splice inputs.version inside double-quoted shell source; lines 62-73 splice version and auth_mode again. GitHub expression substitution occurs before Bash parses the script, so quote, command, or newline metacharacters change program syntax rather than remaining data.
- The affected job has id-token: write and receives NODE_AUTH_TOKEN in the publish step.

Impact

A malicious or mistaken dispatch input can execute shell syntax in a privileged environment. An arbitrary branch/ref can also be packaged as a claimed immutable npm version, breaking source-to-package provenance even when package.json happens to match. Environment approval reduces who can dispatch; it does not make string interpolation safe or make the published ref canonical.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The version equality check does not run before Bash parses the injected source and checkout_ref is deliberately honored. A full tracker search found only the closed npm credential blocker, not input injection or provenance binding.

Proposed fix

Pass all workflow inputs through env fields and quote variable expansions; validate version with an anchored SemVer parser before any use. Remove checkout_ref or require the checked-out commit to equal refs/tags/v$version and the release/tag SHA. Prefer deriving the version from the checked-out signed/protected tag. Split validation from the minimal publication step and minimize secret/OIDC exposure.

Guard tests

- Metacharacter, quote, command-substitution and newline version fixtures are rejected as data without executing markers.
- Valid stable and prerelease SemVer map to the intended npm dist-tag.
- Branch, SHA and mismatched tag refs cannot publish; only the exact v$version tag passes.
- Package version, tag version and release commit must all agree.
- Static workflow lint rejects direct ${{ inputs.* }} interpolation in run blocks.
close_reason: Confirmed workflow-dispatch inputs were interpolated into privileged Bash and arbitrary checkout refs could publish immutable npm versions. Added SemVer/auth validation as data, exact tag checkout plus tag-movement CAS, split unprivileged validation from minimal OIDC publish, compliant dist-tag mapping, injection/static/provenance guards, and focused fmt/clippy/tests.

title: [QA100][P1][security] Restore trusts manifest paths and unauthenticated payloads
description:
Finding

Service restore treats a backup manifest as trusted path and integrity authority. Manifest backup paths may be absolute or contain parent traversal, and the audit source_path is reused as the restore destination outside the state directory. The declared hashes, byte counts, and tree counts are not verified before mutation.

Evidence and reachable paths

- crates/oraclemcp/src/service_lifecycle.rs:1414-1427 accepts an absolute backup_path directly and otherwise joins an unchecked relative path, including parent components, to the chosen backup directory.
- lines 1063-1100 restore config from that path and copy an external audit log to the unauthenticated manifest.audit.source_path.
- lines 801-827 verify only the audit entry, stop the service, then perform the copies.
- lines 1345-1396 accept an absent anchor and treat an empty parsed audit as Verified with zero records.
- crates/oraclemcp-audit/src/verify.rs:100-160 returns Ok for an empty record slice.
- Backup manifests carry SHA-256 and byte metadata, but restore never compares them; sha256_file is used only while creating a manifest.

Attack and impact

A crafted backup directory can set audit.present=true, point audit.backup_path at an empty file inside or outside the bundle, omit the anchor, and set audit.source_path to any operator-writable destination. Verification returns success with zero records. A confirmed restore stops the service and atomically replaces that destination with attacker-chosen bytes. Absolute or parent-traversing config and audit backup paths can also read outside the selected bundle. This is a malicious-backup or supply-chain boundary and can be worse when the service/CLI runs with elevated platform privileges.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The operator explicitly chooses a backup, but the command advertises audit verification before trusting recovered state and therefore must treat the bundle as untrusted input. A second reviewer and a separate source trace confirmed the data flow. The closed backup/restore implementation bead contains no path-containment or manifest-authentication exception, and no duplicate bug exists.

Proposed remediation

- Require every manifest backup_path to be relative and composed only of normal components; canonicalize/open it beneath the canonical backup root with no symlink escape or swap.
- Never use manifest source_path as a write destination. Derive state, config, and audit targets from explicit validated current options/config.
- Preflight the entire manifest before stopping the service. Verify every declared file hash/length and a complete state-file inventory; reject unexpected/missing files.
- Authenticate the manifest or bind its canonical bytes/file inventory into a signature or keyed backup record when provenance is required.
- Do not report an empty audit log as authenticated verification; represent no-record/no-audit state honestly and never let it authorize external-path restoration.

Guard tests

- Reject absolute, parent-traversing, empty, and platform-prefix backup paths.
- Reject audit source_path attempts to redirect the destination.
- Reject symlink and rename-swap escapes between validation and open.
- Empty audit plus absent anchor cannot satisfy an authenticated restore gate.
- Tampered config, audit, state bytes, counts, and hashes fail before the stop command or any write.
- A valid backup still restores to only the explicit state/config/audit targets and preserves audit anchor verification.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed completely. Backup schema v2 now carries an HMAC-authenticated manifest, exact sorted per-state-file SHA-256/length inventory, fixed config/audit/anchor payload paths, and paired signed audit anchor evidence. Restore verifies manifest identity/service/lock policy, exact inventory/counts/bytes/hashes, non-empty signed audit chain and anchor, and stages every verified payload into private anonymous files before any service stop. All source and destination traversal uses no-follow directory capabilities; explicit current state/config/audit targets replace untrusted source_path authority; atomic writes verify directory/file identity and exact final target inventory against symlink, hardlink, and rename swaps. Guard coverage rejects absolute/parent/empty/prefixed paths, unsigned manifest edits, missing/extra/tampered state/config/audit/anchor bytes and counts, empty/unanchored audit, backup-root/file/destination symlinks, hardlink mutation, and source/destination rename replacement; valid explicit restore remains green. Evidence: service_lifecycle 20/20; cargo check -p oraclemcp; clippy -p oraclemcp --all-targets -D warnings; workspace all-target clippy -D warnings; cargo fmt --check; cargo deny; boundary lint all green. Full package run passed 243 lib + 102 bin and all suites except an independently owned QA41 installer metadata fixture failure.

title: [QA100][P1][release] Build Docker rollback from immutable release provenance
description:
Finding

The documented manual Docker rollback/rebuild path accepts a version but always checks out the default branch, then pushes both that version tag and latest from the checked-out source. It neither checks out nor verifies v$version.

Evidence

- .github/workflows/docker.yml:3-13 describes a manual rebuild and accepts version.
- Lines 40-42 checkout the default ref. Lines 52-96 use input only as metadata/tags. Lines 104-107 ask release_preflight to compare current-tree metadata to v$version; lines 118-128 build and push the current tree as the versioned tag plus latest.
- The release rollback documentation instructs operators to dispatch a previous good version. Once main has advanced, that fails preflight; while main still reports the same version, post-tag commits can overwrite an immutable-looking GHCR version with different source.

Impact

The emergency rollback path is unusable after a normal version bump and can otherwise destroy version-tag reproducibility/provenance. Consumers cannot assume ghcr.io/muhdur/oraclemcp:X.Y.Z corresponds to Git tag vX.Y.Z.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is distinct from the normal release workflow, which is tag-triggered. The mismatch is specifically in the manual workflow and its rollback contract. Full tracker search found no Docker rollback/tag-provenance bead.

Proposed fix

For rebuild, resolve and checkout refs/tags/v$version, verify the tag commit and release metadata, and attest that exact SHA. Treat historic version tags as immutable: if the digest already exists, refuse replacement unless it is byte-identical. For rollback, preferably retag only latest to the previously attested digest rather than rebuild/rewrite the historic tag. Record source SHA and digest in job summary.

Guard tests

- With main at the next version, dispatching the previous release resolves its tag and succeeds.
- A missing/mismatched tag or package/server version fails before registry login/push.
- Existing version tag with a different digest is refused.
- Rollback changes latest to the chosen existing digest without rewriting the versioned image.
- Provenance subject/digest and source SHA match the release tag.
close_reason: Confirmed default-branch rebuild could overwrite immutable-looking release tags. Recovery now validates inputs in a read-only job, checks out and verifies the exact vVERSION tag/metadata, makes rollback verify and retag only an existing signed digest, makes rebuild push by digest and refuse any non-identical replacement, source-binds successful rebuild signatures, records SHA/digest provenance, and ships actionlint/static/e2e/docs guards.

title: [QA100][P2][release] Verify artifact bytes before rendering distribution manifests
description:
Finding

The Homebrew/winget renderer trusts the first arbitrary 64-hex substring in each named .sha256 sidecar and never hashes the corresponding archive or requires that the sidecar record names that archive.

Evidence

- scripts/render_distribution_manifests.sh:23-37 uses awk to extract the first 64-hex run from $asset.sha256. It accepts prefixes/suffixes, extra records, a wrong filename and a digest for different bytes.
- Lines 50-69 embed those unverified strings into public package-manager manifests.
- .github/workflows/release.yml invokes this against downloaded tag artifacts; Unix and Windows checksum formats explain the permissive parser but do not bind digest to archive.

Impact

Artifact mixups, malformed sidecars or compromised upload/download state can publish manifests whose checksum describes another file. Users then receive broken installs, or the package metadata authenticates unintended bytes.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is distinct from the installer offline-checksum basename bug in campaign bead .5: this runs at release time and generates Homebrew/winget metadata. Full tracker search found no distribution-renderer checksum bead.

Proposed fix

Hash each downloaded archive directly with sha256sum/shasum -a 256 (or a portable helper) and compare it to a strictly parsed sidecar record that is bound to the expected basename and contains exactly one accepted record. Normalize only documented platform formatting. Render only after every archive/sidecar pair verifies.

Guard tests

- Correct GNU, BSD and explicitly supported Windows checksum formats pass.
- Wrong filename, right digest for another archive, extra record, prefix/suffix junk, missing archive and mutated byte all fail.
- Rendered manifest digest equals a fresh hash of the exact referenced archive.
- Release workflow cannot reach package-manager upload after any verification failure.
close_reason: Confirmed with live pre-fix probes; renderer now recomputes archive SHA-256, strictly validates one basename-bound GNU/BSD/certutil sidecar, fails before rendering/upload on mismatch, and has executable tamper/format/workflow guards.

title: [QA100][P2][SBOM] Preserve integrity and license metadata in freshness checks
description:
Finding

The dashboard SBOM freshness gate deliberately normalizes every dependency component down to bom-ref, name, version and purl. Changes or omissions in hashes, licenses, scope, properties and external references therefore pass as fresh even though those fields are material SBOM content. The merged-artifact check is similarly structural rather than integrity-complete.

Evidence

- scripts/release_sbom_check.sh:52-110 generates a current npm SBOM, then projects components at lines 64-70 and 89-95 to four identity fields before cmp.
- The checked-in dashboard SBOM contains component hashes, license declarations and properties that this comparison ignores. Removing or corrupting them leaves normalized output unchanged.
- Lines 135-152 verify a few merged roots and purl families but do not require hashes/licenses or validate dependency closure.

Impact

A stale or degraded SBOM can pass release gates while omitting the evidence scanners, license review and incident response rely on. Attestation signs the degraded artifact, giving false confidence rather than recovering missing data.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Some top-level npm sbom fields may be nondeterministic and should be normalized, but component integrity/license fields are not noise. Full tracker search found prior general SBOM delivery work, not this field-loss/freshness bug.

Proposed fix

Compare a documented canonical CycloneDX projection that preserves all stable component security/legal metadata and dependency edges, removing only proven nondeterministic generator/timestamp fields. Validate against the CycloneDX schema, require hashes where npm emits them, and verify every dependency ref resolves to metadata/component or a component. Keep a reviewed exception list for genuinely unstable fields.

Guard tests

- Mutating/removing a dependency hash, license, scope, property or external reference makes --source fail.
- Timestamp/tool-version-only normalization behaves as documented.
- Duplicate/dangling dependency refs and schema-invalid merged artifacts fail.
- A freshly generated canonical SBOM passes deterministically on repeated runs.
close_reason: SBOM freshness preserves integrity/license metadata. release_sbom_check.sh normalized components to {bom-ref,name,version,purl} before cmp, dropping hashes/licenses/scope/properties/externalRefs, and had no dependency-closure check. Replaced with a canonical projection (del serialNumber/metadata.timestamp/metadata.tools + deep array sort + jq -S) preserving every stable component security/legal field + dep edges, so a mutated/dropped hash or license now fails the freshness cmp; added check_closure (unique bom-refs; every dependency ref resolves) to --source and --artifact; added validate_schema (real validator if present else jq schema-lite w/ NOTE). New guard-test script (8 offline checks) proves mutated hash/license/scope + serialNumber/timestamp/tool normalization + closure/dup/schema-invalid detection. Offline limit noted: live --source freshness run + external schema validation need npm + web/node_modules + a validator (absent here) — degraded gracefully with NOTEs. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P2][custom-tools] Sign every semantic and agent-visible definition field
description:
Finding

Protected-profile custom-tool HMAC signatures omit every parameter description and output_mode from their canonical bytes. Parameter descriptions are emitted verbatim into the MCP input schema, so an actor who can alter tools.toml but lacks the signing key can change agent-facing instructions while the signature still verifies.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/custom_tools.rs:381-406 signs name, top-level description, body, level and each param name/type/required flag only.
- Lines 256-276 copy ParamDef.description into agent-visible JSON Schema.
- CustomToolDef.output_mode at lines 69-100 is parsed semantic metadata but absent from canonical_bytes.
- enforce_signature then accepts the unchanged HMAC on a protected profile.

Impact

The signature claims file integrity but permits unsigned prompt/instruction changes in parameter descriptions. That can steer an agent toward unsafe values or disclosure while operators see a valid signature. Omitted output metadata also makes the format brittle when shaping is implemented.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Descriptions are not comments: they are part of MCP discovery consumed by the model. The protected-profile threat model explicitly treats a tools-file writer without the HMAC key as untrusted. Full tracker search found no field-coverage bead.

Proposed fix

Define a versioned canonical serialization covering every semantic and agent-visible field except signature, preferably canonical serde with an explicit domain/version. Provide a deliberate migration/re-sign command and reject ambiguous legacy signatures on protected profiles after the documented transition.

Guard tests

- Sign once, then independently flip name, descriptions at every nesting level, body, param type/order/required, output_mode and level; every mutation fails verification.
- Signature field alone is excluded.
- Canonical vectors are stable across platforms and map ordering.
- Version migration is explicit and protected startup never silently downgrades.
close_reason: Confirmed: protected-profile HMAC omitted nested descriptions and output_mode. Replaced with versioned exhaustive canonical signing, explicit legacy re-sign failure, documentation, mutation guards, and stable vectors.

title: [QA100][P3][custom-tools] Implement or remove the accepted no-op output_mode
description:
Finding

Custom-tool definitions parse and document output_mode values rows and scalar, but production execution never reads the field. output_mode = scalar silently returns the unchanged row-oriented result.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/custom_tools.rs:69-100 defines and accepts OutputMode.
- execute_custom_tool at lines 578-594 binds then delegates without inspecting it.
- The production ReadOnlyCustomToolExecutor in crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:4906 onward returns the normal query result. A repository-wide Rust search finds output_mode only in definitions, docs and fixtures, not execution.

Impact

Operators configure a supported-looking contract that does nothing; agents receive a different shape than advertised and automation can fail late.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is not merely reserved syntax: the enum has user-facing documentation and TOML examples. No tracker bead records it as intentionally future-only or unsupported.

Proposed fix

Either implement strict scalar shaping with a documented zero-row, one-row/one-column and multi-row/column contract, or remove the option and reject it until supported. Update catalog/schema/robot docs so accepted configuration equals behavior.

Guard tests

- Scalar mode covers zero, exactly one and more-than-one rows/columns with typed outcomes.
- Rows mode remains unchanged.
- If removed, parsing output_mode=scalar fails with an actionable unsupported-field error.
- Discovery and execution agree on the output contract.
close_reason: Implement-or-remove the accepted no-op output_mode. output_mode=scalar parsed and was documented, but production execution (execute_custom_tool / ReadOnlyCustomToolExecutor::run) never inspected it and silently returned the row set — accepted config that did nothing. Chose the fail-closed 'reject until supported' option (scalar shaping is an unimplemented execution feature; P3): CustomToolDef::validate() now rejects output_mode=scalar with an actionable LoadError::Invalid ('output_mode = scalar is not yet supported; use rows'), so a scalar definition is refused at load (accepted configuration == behavior). Updated the OutputMode::Scalar doc to say Not-yet-supported; updated the two signature-stability fixtures (which used scalar arbitrarily) to rows and re-pinned the canonical signature (algorithm unchanged; field-order-stability assertion still guards canonicalization). New test: scalar rejected at load with 'scalar' reason, rows still loads. No scalar usage in the binary crate. Green: core custom_tools 42/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P1][custom-tools] Refresh MCP discovery from the active profile catalog
description:
Finding

Profile switching replaces the dispatcher custom-tool catalog and emits tools/list_changed, but tools/list, capabilities and other discovery remain bound to the catalog registered at process startup. Newly active tools can be callable only by guessing while stale tools remain advertised and then fail as unknown.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:2103-2109 registers options.custom_catalog into a static ToolRegistry and builds capabilities once.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:5461-5522 loads and commits a new state.custom_catalog on oracle_switch_profile and explicitly emits tools/list_changed.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:649-693 filters self.registry for visible descriptors. McpSurfaceState carries level/profile state but no active custom descriptors/catalog generation.
- Dispatch later resolves custom calls from the replaced state.custom_catalog, so discovery and execution use different snapshots.

Impact

After profile switch, clients obeying the list_changed notification still cannot discover the new tools and may offer removed ones. This breaks per-profile isolation and can mislead agents about available operations.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The notification proves dynamic discovery is intended. A full tracker search found only closed generic custom-tool/profile work, no stale startup registry bug.

Proposed fix

Carry an immutable active catalog snapshot or generation in McpSurfaceState/lane state. Render tools/list, oracle://tools and capabilities from built-ins plus that exact active snapshot, and resolve dispatch from the same generation. Keep catalogs isolated by stateful/stateless HTTP lane and principal.

Guard tests

- Start profile A with custom_a, switch to B with custom_b, observe list_changed, then assert tools/list removes A/adds B and both capabilities/resources agree.
- custom_b executes and custom_a refuses after switch.
- Stdio, stateful HTTP and stateless HTTP cover lane/principal isolation and concurrent switches.
- A failed switch leaves both discovery and execution on the old snapshot.
close_reason: Verified by failing-before/fixed-after profile-switch regression. Discovery and dispatch now share one immutable lane-local custom catalog generation; tools/list, oracle_capabilities, oracle://tools, startup wiring, failure atomicity, list_changed, stateful principal/session isolation, and stateless control-lane isolation are covered by focused tests. fmt, affected full test suites, and all-target clippy pass.

title: [QA100][P2][custom-tools] Validate declared params against SQL bind placeholders at load time
description:
Finding

Custom-tool loading validates parameter identifiers and uniqueness but never verifies that the declared parameter set matches the named bind placeholders in sql or call. Broken definitions load, are advertised, and fail only on every Oracle invocation.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/custom_tools.rs:205-253 validates the body, param identifier/duplicates and level only.
- bind_params at lines 507-550 blindly constructs one bind for every declaration.
- execute_custom_tool at lines 578-594 passes those binds and body unchanged to Oracle. A typo such as body :customer_id with only param id therefore reaches ORA-01008/ORA-01036; a declared unused bind can also mismatch.

Impact

Catalog misconfiguration becomes a permanently advertised but unusable tool and consumes database round trips to discover a deterministic local error.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The docs state params are bind variables referenced by name and no dynamic/bind-superset contract is documented. Full tracker search found no placeholder-contract bead.

Proposed fix

At load time, extract Oracle named binds with a SQL-aware lexer, ignoring quoted identifiers/literals/comments and := assignment. Normalize Oracle bind-name case and require an exact set, or explicitly define and validate any allowed unused-parameter policy. Reuse the guard lexer if it can expose tokens without weakening classification.

Guard tests

- Missing, extra and case-duplicate declarations fail load with tool/param context.
- Repeated placeholders and case-insensitive matches pass.
- Colons inside ordinary/q/national strings, quoted identifiers, line/block comments and PL/SQL := do not create binds.
- Both inline SQL and package-call forms are covered; invalid entries never appear in discovery.
close_reason: Validate declared custom-tool params against SQL named binds at load time. Added oraclemcp_guard::named_bind_placeholders() (pure projection over the existing sqlparser Tokenizer/OracleDialect — a bare Colon immediately followed by an unquoted Word; colons in strings/q-quotes/quoted-idents/comments, := assignment, :: cast, and whitespace-separated colons are all excluded; positional/quoted binds skipped; names uppercased+deduped for Oracle case-insensitivity; tokenizer failure fails closed to empty set). CustomToolDef::validate() now requires exact set-equality between declared params and body binds (rejects typo'd/undeclared binds AND declared-but-unused params before tools/list ever advertises them), plus a case-insensitive duplicate-param check. Strictly a load/config-path tightening; no query-path guard verdict touched. Integrated onto current main via 3-way merge (agent built on a stale base): classifier.rs additive fn applied clean; resolved lib.rs re-export to keep both named_bind_placeholders + semantic_read_plan; kept the write-refusal test as static DML with the undeclared :id dropped (id=7). Green on main: guard 153/0, core lib 557/0, fmt+clippy(guard,core) clean.

title: [QA100][P2][redaction] Keep trusted session SQL literals out of connection and doctor errors
description:
Finding

Trusted session setup statements may contain tenant/token context literals, and Oracle can echo attacker/operator-chosen error text, but neither driver-error sanitization nor doctor redaction treats those statements as sensitive. Startup and online-doctor errors can therefore expose configured literals verbatim.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-config/src/profile.rs:511-514 permits arbitrary trusted local session statements; README documents profile-owner PL/SQL for application contexts.
- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs:1002-1015 executes each opts.session_statements during connect. execute_raw at 1412-1435 wraps sanitize_driver_error output.
- sanitize_driver_error at 4101-4154 inventories credentials, wallet, app context and identity but omits session_statements.
- OracleConnectOptions::doctor_redaction_values in crates/oraclemcp-db/src/types.rs:827-882 has the same omission; doctor only exact-scrubs supplied values.
- An existing debug-redaction test uses DBMS_SESSION.SET_CONTEXT with secret-token, confirming such literals are sensitive. A live local Oracle raise_application_error probe echoed its supplied sentinel in ORA-20000.

Impact

Operator-configured tenant identifiers, tokens or context material can leak into startup/doctor JSON, logs or handoffs after a setup failure. Trigger requires local configuration or a server-side error path, so this is P2 rather than remote injection.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The statements are trusted code but their literal data is still secret. Driver errors can legally include application error text. Full  tracker search found no session-setup error-redaction bead.

Proposed fix

For session-setup failures, expose structured ORA code/class and statement ordinal, not raw server message or SQL. Generic suppression is safer than trying to tokenize PL/SQL because it can synthesize secrets. Ensure all startup, pool and online-doctor paths share the policy.

Guard tests

- Fake/live session statement raises an error containing a quoted sentinel; DbError, logs, doctor text and JSON omit it while retaining ORA code and statement index.
- Multiple statements identify the failing ordinal without SQL.
- Existing credential/topology redaction remains intact.
- Successful setup is unaffected.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed: trusted session setup failures now suppress all SQL/server detail while retaining the failing statement ordinal and structured ORA code/class; startup, pool, lane stub, tool, and online-doctor paths share the driver-adapter policy. Regression tests cover secret/SQL omission, multiple-statement ordinal, known and unknown ORA codes, no-code errors, JSON, and unchanged success. Full oraclemcp-db tests and clippy pass.

title: [QA100][P2][pool] Remove concrete OraclePool mutation escape hatch
description:
Finding

OraclePool implements the stateless OracleConnection contract by explicitly refusing execute/commit/rollback, yet the same public concrete type exposes an inherent public execute method that runs arbitrary DML/DDL and returns the connection to the idle pool without commit or rollback. Rust method resolution selects this escape hatch for concrete callers.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/pool.rs:281-295 exposes OraclePool::execute and delegates to the underlying connection.
- with_conn at 333-363 regards a successful call as clean after ping; checkin at 453-465 pushes it back to idle. Oracle DML may still have an open transaction.
- impl OracleConnection for OraclePool at 511-553 deliberately refuses execute, commit and rollback because it is a stateless metadata pool.
- Current binary boxes the pool as dyn OracleConnection, so the shipped path is protected, but oraclemcp-db public consumers and future concrete call sites are not. A closed session-safety bead claimed OraclePool fails execute, missing this inherent API.

Impact

A concrete caller can leave uncommitted DML or session state on a connection later leased for unrelated metadata work, violating isolation and the rollback-default architecture. There is no corresponding inherent commit/rollback cleanup contract.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The two methods have intentionally contradictory contracts, not overloads for different pool modes. Repo production currently uses the safe trait-object path, so this is P2 latent/public-API risk rather than a present binary exploit. Tracker search found no follow-up to the incompletely satisfied closed bead.

Proposed fix

Delete/private the inherent mutation method or make it return the identical structured stateless refusal. Prefer a read-only pool capability/type that cannot expose mutation at compile time. If a transactional pool is ever needed, give it a separate lease/transaction type with mandatory rollback-on-drop/return semantics.

Guard tests

- Concrete and trait-object calls both refuse before backend invocation.
- API/compile-surface test prevents a public mutating method on the stateless pool.
- Live max_size=1 test proves DML and ALTER SESSION state cannot be parked and observed by the next borrower.
- Query/ping/describe pooling behavior remains green.
close_reason: Confirmed: public inherent OraclePool::execute bypassed the same type's OracleConnection mutation refusal and returned successful DML/DDL sessions to the stateless pool without transaction ownership. Removed the inherent mutation API, updated the locked public-API baseline, and added a concrete-plus-trait-object regression proving both refuse before any checkout/accounting. Full oraclemcp-db suite (333 unit plus integration/contract/golden/trybuild), all-target clippy, fmt, API lock, and diff checks pass.

title: [QA100][P3][http] Never serialize unknown error statuses with an OK reason phrase
description:
Finding

The native HTTP writer emits HTTP/1.1 499 OK for cancelled requests because status 499 is generated but reason_phrase maps every unknown status to OK. Future unmapped error statuses inherit the same lie.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:5355-5369 returns numeric 499 for cancellation.
- reason_phrase at 6338-6357 has no 499 arm and defaults to OK.
- Existing cancellation tests assert only the in-memory numeric status, not served wire bytes.

Impact

Proxies, raw clients and access logs receive a contradictory success phrase for a cancellation, complicating incident diagnosis and any parser that exposes the phrase.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Reason phrases are advisory in modern HTTP but this server deliberately writes one and OK is semantically wrong. Full tracker search found no status-line mapping bead.

Proposed fix

Map 499 to Client Closed Request if retaining the de-facto code, or use a documented standard status. Change the fallback to Unknown Status or reject unmapped statuses so errors can never default to success text.

Guard tests

- Served-wire cancellation asserts the exact status line.
- Every status produced by the server has an explicit phrase.
- Synthetic unknown status never contains OK.
- Existing JSON cancellation body and numeric status remain stable.
close_reason: Confirmed: cancellation generated numeric 499 while the native HTTP writer defaulted every unmapped status to OK. Added explicit 499 Client Closed Request, made unknown fallback Unknown Status, and added wire-level guards for cancellation, every emitted status mapping, and a synthetic unknown status that can never serialize as OK. Full oraclemcp-core library suite (482/482 including concurrent in-flight work), fmt, and diff checks pass.

title: [QA100][P1][installer] Offline checksum can verify a different file than the archive
description:
Finding

The Bash installer passes only the checksum sidecar to sha256sum or shasum check mode. Those tools trust the filename embedded in the sidecar, so success does not prove that the selected release archive was hashed.

Evidence and reachable path

- install.sh:838-850 verify_checksum accepts one checksum path, changes to its directory, and runs the check tool on the sidecar. It never receives the archive path.
- install.sh:917-937 require_offline_bundle verifies only the expected archive basename and the presence/readability of sidecar files.
- install.sh:1114-1150 then calls verify_checksum on the sidecar, optionally soft-skips cosign under the default prefer posture when cosign is absent, and extracts the unverified archive.
- A direct reproduction using a sidecar record with the known empty digest and filename /dev/null returns /dev/null: OK even while the candidate archive has a different digest.
- install.ps1:375-392 correctly derives an expected digest then hashes the Archive argument directly, so the Windows path is not affected.

Impact

An offline bundle can print the hard SHA-256 verified success path while no digest was computed for the archive that is subsequently extracted and installed. Accidental sidecar/archive mixups and a tampered sidecar that names any matching local file bypass the required transport-integrity check. With default prefer and no cosign, no later authenticity check catches it.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Official sidecars normally contain the expected basename, but offline mode explicitly accepts local bundle inputs and the installer promises a mandatory archive digest check. Trusting conventional sidecar contents is not equivalent to verifying the selected path. Two reviewers reproduced or traced the behavior. Existing installer verification beads test a mismatched digest for the normal filename but not filename substitution; no duplicate issue exists.

Proposed remediation

Parse the expected digest from a strict sidecar format and compute SHA-256 over the archive argument directly. Require exactly one record whose filename equals the archive basename, reject absolute paths, separators, parent components, missing filenames, malformed digests, and extra records. Keep dual sha256sum and shasum support, but use them to hash the explicit archive rather than delegate path selection to sidecar content.

Guard tests

- A valid one-record sidecar for the exact archive basename passes on Linux and macOS tooling.
- A sidecar naming /dev/null, another archive, an absolute path, or a parent path fails.
- Extra-record and digest-only ambiguity fails closed according to the documented format.
- Wrong digest for the correct basename still fails.
- Default prefer without cosign cannot reach extraction unless the explicit archive digest matched.
- PowerShell direct-archive checksum tests remain green.
close_reason: Confirmed by direct /dev/null filename-substitution reproduction. verify_checksum now strictly parses exactly one basename-bound GNU SHA-256 record, hashes the selected archive path directly with sha256sum or shasum, normalizes and compares digests, and fails before cosign/extraction/install on malformed, aliased, extra-record, or mismatched sidecars. Offline smoke guards /dev/null, wrong/absolute/parent names, digest-only, extra records, mismatch, no-install-on-failure, real sha256sum, and forced shasum fallback; Bash syntax, shellcheck, installer smoke, exact Rust e2e, fmt, clippy, and diff checks pass.

title: [QA100][P1][dashboard] Decode forwarded MCP failures before rendering success
description:
Finding

The operator edge returns HTTP 200 whenever a JSON-RPC response was forwarded, even when that response is an RPC error or MCP tool result with isError=true. The dashboard treats any 2xx as success, renders a green ok badge and performs success side effects such as clearing metadata cache. Change-proposal apply similarly marks stopped_on_failure as ok.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:4626-4648 sets status=forwarded and HTTP 200 based only on response presence, embedding mcp_response without decoding its outcome.
- web/src/app/operator-client.ts:1334-1357 rejects only non-2xx HTTP.
- web/src/app/App.tsx:6067-6129 sets Workbench/IDE state ok unconditionally; WorkbenchResultPanel at 7068-7127 derives a green badge solely from that state. Commit then clears metadata cache as if mutation landed.
- Proposal apply onSuccess at 5208-5236 also always sets ok, while the server can honestly return a 200 domain result with status stopped_on_failure and per-statement failures.

Impact

Bad/consumed confirmations, level refusals, Oracle failures, timeouts and partial proposal application can appear green to an operator. This is dangerous truthfulness failure at the human safety boundary and may trigger invalid cache/state transitions.

False-positive and duplicate checks

HTTP 200 is a valid envelope status for JSON-RPC, so the server need not change it; the bug is treating transport success as operation success. Campaign audit bead .20 covers durable audit terminal outcomes, not dashboard rendering. Full tracker search found no UI equivalent.

Proposed fix

Create one typed outcome decoder covering HTTP status, JSON-RPC error, MCP result.isError/error envelope and route-specific domain statuses. Represent success, refused, failed and partial/stopped distinctly; show message/next steps. Only run invalidation/clear-success effects after authoritative mutation success.

Guard tests

- UI fixtures for HTTP200+JSON-RPC error, HTTP200+isError, stopped_on_failure and partial apply render warning/error, expose useful detail and do not run success side effects.
- Refusal/challenge-required is visually distinct from internal failure.
- True read/preview/execute/apply successes remain green.
- Server/client contract test locks the decoder against representative MCP envelopes.
close_reason: Verified HTTP 200 transport success masked JSON-RPC, MCP isError, and stopped proposal outcomes. Added a typed semantic decoder at the shared operator client boundary; refused, failed, and partial responses now reject before success callbacks while retaining redacted detail for distinct UI rendering. Success-only cache invalidation is guarded by promise settlement. Added red-first UI/client fixtures and a real Rust server contract test; focused web, core, fmt, and clippy gates pass.

title: [QA100][P3][dashboard] Coalesce concurrent Explorer cache misses and account replacements
description:
Finding

Two concurrent Explorer metadata misses for the same key both load and then add their byte count, while the second Map.set replaces the first entry without subtracting its bytes. A slower stale load can also overwrite a newer value.

Evidence

- web/src/app/operator-client.ts:1175-1208 checks the Map before await load, then unconditionally sets and increments explorerMetadataCacheBytes.
- removeExplorerCacheEntry at 1411-1418 is the only normal subtraction path, but replacement bypasses it.
- trimExplorerMetadataCache at 1420-1438 trusts the inflated counter for eviction.

Impact

The cache summary lies and valid unrelated entries can be evicted prematurely; out-of-order resolution can restore stale metadata.

False-positive and duplicate checks

JavaScript is single-threaded but promises interleave across the await boundary, so both misses can observe absence. Full tracker search found no same-key cache-race bead.

Proposed fix

Coalesce an in-flight promise per key. Before any replacement subtract the current entry, and use a generation/token so an older loader cannot overwrite a newer invalidation/load. Keep counters derived or invariant-checked.

Guard tests

- Two deferred same-key loads resolved out of order perform one load and retain the intended newest value.
- Reported bytes equal actual serialized resident entries after replacement/invalidation.
- No unrelated entry is evicted under the byte ceiling.
- Rejected load clears its in-flight slot and retry works.
close_reason: Confirmed with two same-key promises crossing the await boundary: both loaders ran, replaced one Map entry, double-counted bytes, and could evict unrelated metadata. Added per-key in-flight coalescing, cache-generation invalidation, generation-safe cleanup, replacement subtraction, and completion-time TTLs. Tests prove one load, exact resident byte accounting, unrelated-entry retention, old-generation out-of-order completion cannot overwrite new data, and rejection clears the retry slot. TypeScript, focused/full Vitest (21/21), production build/SBOM/bundle check, and diff checks pass.

title: [QA100][P1][http] Put byte budgets on stateful MCP response replay
description:
Finding

The stateful HTTP replay store is count-bounded to 128 JSON responses per session but has no per-entry, per-session or global byte accounting. One query page may be 25 MiB, so one session can retain roughly 3.2 GiB before count eviction; multiple sessions multiply it.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:993-1070 stores Value payloads in a per-session Vec and prunes only by event count.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:84-87 permits a 25 MiB serialized oracle_query page; other source/DDL outputs can also be multi-megabyte.
- Existing expiry/replay tests use tiny sequence fixtures and assert counts, not resident bytes.
- Planned aggregate session-count bounds do not make a single live session safe.

Impact

An authenticated caller can drive multi-gigabyte resident memory and process OOM through otherwise valid bounded-result calls. Replay cloning further amplifies transient memory.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The 128 event cap is real but irrelevant to payload size. This is distinct from campaign bead .17 on unbounded session registries and .23 on idempotency eviction order. Full tracker search found no replay byte-budget issue.

Proposed fix

Add deterministic per-entry, per-session/principal and global byte ceilings with exact/defensible serialized-size accounting and oldest-completed eviction. Oversized responses should be explicitly non-replayable or represented by a bounded gap/reference with honest resume semantics. Avoid cloning large Values on replay where possible.

Guard tests

- Large payloads keep resident accounting below every cap for one and many sessions.
- Small exact replay still works.
- Oversized response behavior is typed and produces honest stream-gap/retry guidance.
- Concurrent append/replay/remove cannot exceed global accounting or underflow counters.
- Memory benchmark covers cloning/transient peak, not only retained size.
close_reason: Confirmed: replay retention was count-only and could hold 128 multi-megabyte Values per session. Added production per-entry (4 MiB), per-session (16 MiB), and global (64 MiB) serialized-byte budgets with exact locked accounting, monotonic per-session sequence state, deterministic oldest-completion global eviction, bounded typed gap markers for oversized responses, dropped-through cursor truth even when a session is fully evicted, and Arc-backed payloads so replay clones are cheap. Configurable-limit tests prove oversized bodies are absent, all caps and accounting hold under concurrent append/remove, unrelated sessions are globally evicted oldest-first, and expired resume is explicit. Focused behavioral tests and core compile pass; broad core gate is batched with the concurrent QA9 fix.

title: [QA100][P1][config] Make rollback compare-and-swap and single-consumer
description:
Finding

Config apply is compare-and-swap against the staged base, but rollback can reuse any retained apply report and blindly overwrite whatever config is current. The rollback ID is cloned before I/O and consumed afterward, so concurrent callers can both execute it.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:304-319 correctly locks and checks current SHA for apply.
- ConfigOpsService::apply at 408-438 retains every successful ConfigApplyReport in rollback_reports.
- rollback at 441-453 clones a report, derives a reverse plan from current bytes, then calls rollback_applied_config.
- Backend rollback at 332-353 acquires the service lock but never verifies current target SHA equals report.applied_sha256 before restoring the older backup.
- Sequence apply A, apply B, rollback A restores pre-A over B. An out-of-band edit is likewise destroyed, and two concurrent rollback-A calls can both clone before removal.

Impact

A stale one-click rollback can clobber a newer reviewed config and reintroduce old ceilings, credentials or exposure policy. The emitted reload plan may be derived from bytes different from the ones actually replaced.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Rollback is expected to mutate, but safe apply already establishes the intended optimistic-concurrency contract. A full tracker search found rollback docs/release work, not ConfigOps stale-ID/CAS handling.

Proposed fix

Atomically claim the rollback ID. Under the same config-ops service lock, verify target SHA equals report.applied_sha256/current generation, read the exact locked bytes, derive the reverse reload plan, restore, and consume the claim only after success. Define whether a later apply invalidates all older IDs. Failed rollback should remain safely retryable only while the expected generation still matches.

Guard tests

- A then B then rollback A is rejected with no write.
- External edit causes CurrentChanged and preserves bytes.
- Two concurrent same-ID rollbacks yield exactly one success.
- Rollback B succeeds and its reload plan matches the exact restored bytes.
- I/O/reload failure has documented retry semantics.
- A security-lowering apply cannot be undone by an older stale ID.
close_reason: Confirmed stale-generation overwrite with a failing regression. Rollback now CAS-checks applied_sha256 and derives reload snapshots under the file-store lock, newer applies invalidate older IDs, IDs are atomically claimed and restored on pre-write failure, and live reload refusal has explicit restart-required semantics. Added external-edit, supersession, 32-caller single-consumer, exact-reload, I/O retry, reload refusal, and security-ceiling guards.

title: [QA100][P2][dashboard] Page, index, and retain Reviews stores
description:
Finding

Reviews storage grows append-only, while dashboard polling repeatedly scans/materializes the entire proposal and source-history corpus. source-history max_rows truncates only after every file/line is read, parsed and globally sorted; proposal listing has no limit/cursor and includes full SQL templates.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/source_history.rs:113-153 reads every history JSONL file fully, parses all matching records, sorts them, then truncates.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:3602-3615 clamps output max_rows but cannot bound store work.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/change_proposal.rs:40-64 reads every proposal file, materializes every full view and sorts with no pagination/retention. Proposals are not removed.
- web/src/app/operator-client.ts:870-875 fetches all proposals and 100 history items; App.tsx:5144-5153 polls them every 10s/15s even when unchanged.

Impact

Normal long-lived use permanently increases disk I/O, parsing, allocation and response size; authorized operators can accelerate it with near-request-cap drafts. Eventually simply opening Reviews becomes an availability problem.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Output history is capped, but work before truncation is not. Proposal responses are uncapped. Full unlimited tracker search found feature beads but no pagination/retention issue.

Proposed fix

Maintain bounded/indexed metadata or tail/reverse-read per-object history so filters/limits apply during streaming. Add stable cursor pagination, status filters and documented close/archive/retention for proposals. Keep large SQL bodies out of list views and fetch detail on selection. Replace unchanged polling with ETag/version or event invalidation.

Guard tests

- Large stores prove max_rows=100 deserializes/allocates O(page), not O(total).
- Proposal list response/body and resident bytes remain bounded.
- Newest-first cursors are stable under concurrent appends with no skips/duplicates.
- Malformed/oversized single records fail locally rather than hiding the whole board.
- UI transfers only changed bounded pages and detail loading remains explicit.
close_reason: Page, index, and retain the Reviews stores — HTTP+frontend activation of the store groundwork (committed at 1646f76). ChangeProposalsList now: store.etag() -> 304+ETag on matching If-None-Match, else store.list_page(cursor) -> 200 {source, proposals(stripped: sql_sha256 kept, sql_template dropped), nextCursor} + ETag header. New ChangeProposalsDetail route (/change-proposals/{id}, single-segment, excludes draft/apply) -> store.detail(id) returns the full sql_template on selection; unknown id -> 404. SourceHistoryList: same etag/If-None-Match/304 + history.list_page(filter, cursor) + nextCursor + ETag. UI contract fixtures regenerated (UPDATE_OPERATOR_SCHEMA=1): CP list drops sql_template, both add nextCursor:null (generated schema/TS byte-identical). Frontend (web/): operatorGet sends If-None-Match + treats 304 as unchanged (skip re-render) + captures ETag; list types stripped + nextCursor; fetchChangeProposalDetail(id) fetches the body on selection; cursor pagers on both boards. Green on main: core lib 605/0, CP/SH http tests 12/0, fmt+clippy clean; frontend agent-verified (tsc --noEmit 0, vite build OK, vitest 25/25). Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P1][oauth] Reject JWTs that are not explicitly access tokens
description:
Finding

The OAuth resource server describes RFC 9068 JWT access-token validation but discards the JWT typ header and accepts any correctly signed JWT whose issuer, audience, time and scopes happen to match. This leaves a cross-JWT substitution boundary open.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/oauth_rs.rs:98-149 parses only alg, claims, signing input and signature, then checks iss/aud/exp/nbf/scopes.
- parse_jwt at 188-203 reads the header but returns only alg, losing typ.
- Tests at 325-358 mint typ=JWT, omit sub/client_id/iat/jti, and call that a valid token.
- The implementation comment at line 132 explicitly invokes RFC 9068 for required exp, establishing the intended token profile. RFC 9068 section 4 requires a resource server to accept typ only at+jwt or application/at+jwt and reject every other value to prevent ID-token confusion.

Impact

Where an issuer reuses keys and claims/audiences across token classes, a signed ID token or other JWT can be substituted for an access token and receive Oracle scope authorization. Audience checks reduce common cases but do not provide explicit token-class separation.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is not a request to infer token type heuristically: the standard provides an explicit protected header. A full tracker search for typ, at+jwt, token confusion and RFC 9068 found only generic closed OAuth work.

Proposed fix

Parse and validate typ before claims: accept only case-insensitive at+jwt/application/at+jwt per the standard and reject missing/other types. Validate the required access-token claim shapes the project claims to support (including nonempty sub/client_id/jti and numeric iat if declaring full RFC 9068 conformance), or explicitly document a separate legacy generic-JWT mode that is opt-in and isolated from the standards-conformant default.

Guard tests

- Same valid signature/iss/aud/exp/scope with typ JWT, missing typ or an ID-token-like header/claims is rejected.
- at+jwt and application/at+jwt pass with compliant claims.
- Missing/malformed required claims fail with a non-leaking invalid_token outcome.
- Header duplicate/type-confusion and case handling follow the chosen standard precisely.
- Existing OAuth HTTP and scope-ceiling tests use compliant access tokens.
close_reason: Confirmed: the validator accepted signed generic typ=JWT tokens despite enforcing RFC 9068-derived access-token claims. Enforced at+jwt/application/at+jwt before signature/claims, rejected duplicate protected headers, validated required RFC 9068 claim shapes, preserved opaque HTTP invalid_token failures, updated OAuth fixtures/docs, and passed full workspace fmt/clippy/test/deny gates.

title: [QA100][P2][oauth] Bind lane principals to both subject and client identity
description:
Finding

OAuth principal derivation hashes issuer plus only the first available identity claim. Because sub wins, two clients acting for the same user collapse to one MCP principal even when client_id/authorized party differs. Stateful session ownership, lane state and grant generation then cannot distinguish those clients.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:1447-1460 selects the first of sub, client_id, azp rather than composing the identity tuple.
- The resulting principal key drives rate buckets, stateless single-principal admission, stateful session owner checks, dispatch context, lane close/revocation and operator subject hashing throughout the same module.
- Existing OAuth token tests omit client_id, so cross-client same-sub behavior is not exercised.

Impact

Two separately authorized applications for the same issuer/user can resume/share the same stateful identity and lane/profile/elevation context. Per-request scope still lowers each call, so this is cross-client state and grant isolation failure, not a direct scope-ceiling bypass.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Stable identity across refreshed tokens is desirable, but OAuth client identity is part of the credential/authorized-party boundary. Combining it does not require hashing the whole token. Full tracker search found no same-sub cross-client isolation bead.

Proposed fix

After standards-compliant token validation, derive the lane principal from a canonical tuple such as issuer + subject + client_id/azp, with explicit rules for machine/client-credentials grants. Keep a separate human/audit subject if user-centric grouping is needed. Never derive security identity from unvalidated claims; return typed validated identity from the auth crate.

Guard tests

- Valid tokens with same iss/sub and client A versus B produce different principals, and B cannot resume/close A session or consume A grants.
- Refreshed tokens with the same tuple but different jti/expiry/scope remain the same principal while scope is enforced per request.
- client-credentials and delegated-user grants follow documented tuple rules.
- Principal material is hashed/redacted in responses and logs.
close_reason: Bind OAuth lane principals to subject AND client identity. oauth_principal_key_from_validated_token did find_map over [sub, client_id, azp] returning only the FIRST present claim, so two different clients (distinct client_id/azp) acting for the same subject collapsed to one principal key -> cross-client session/revocation bleed. Now composes iss with EVERY present identity claim in fixed order (iss + sub + client_id + azp), so distinct (subject, client) tuples get distinct keys; falls back to token-hash only when none of sub/client_id/azp is present (unchanged). Sub-only tokens keep their existing key (no regression to the refresh-stability test). New test: same subject + different client_id -> different principals; same for azp; identical iss+sub+client+azp -> one stable principal; different subjects stay distinct. Green: core oauth_principal 2/2, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P1][recovery] Preserve quoted identifier identity in source snapshots
description:
Finding

The pre-DDL recovery snapshot parser strips quotes and uppercases a quoted object name, rebinding it to a different Oracle object. Applying CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE "foo" can snapshot FOO while modifying the distinct case-sensitive object "foo".

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/source_history.rs:362-376 passes the name through clean_source_name_token and uppercases every parsed part.
- clean_source_name_token at 416-427 removes surrounding quotes when the remainder is a simple identifier.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:3201-3281 forwards the transformed target to oracle_get_source/oracle_get_ddl before dispatching the original SQL.
- If FOO and "foo" coexist, snapshot fetch succeeds for FOO and reports captured, then apply changes "foo". Existing parser tests at source_history.rs:499-521 cover only unquoted identifiers.

Impact

The Reviews recovery UI can claim a valid pre-change snapshot while the stored revert artifact belongs to another object. A later rollback can overwrite the wrong source and cannot recover the actual target.

False-positive and duplicate checks
