Oracle quoted identifiers preserve spelling/case and are distinct from uppercased unquoted names. This is separate from campaign bead .3, which concerns confirmation-token digest collisions. Full tracker search found no source-snapshot quoted-identifier issue.

Proposed fix

Use the Oracle lexer/parser/token model already used by guard/DDL tooling, preserving owner/name spelling plus quote flags end to end into fetch metadata and target binding. Until that is correct, fail closed to status skipped for any quoted identifier rather than normalize it. Bind the captured source target digest/identity to the exact apply target and verify before dispatch.

Guard tests

- Coexisting FOO and "foo" prove only the exact target is fetched, snapshotted and reverted.
- Mixed-case quoted owner/name, escaped quotes, comments and edition clauses retain identity.
- Unsupported/ambiguous parse reports skipped, never captured.
- Captured target identity/digest must equal the apply target before execution.
- Unquoted Oracle uppercase normalization remains correct.
close_reason: Confirmed Oracle identity rebinding: quote-aware/comment-aware source target parsing now preserves spelling and quote flags; fetch metadata and fetched source text must match the apply target's length-framed SHA-256 identity before persistence; schema v2 stores quote flags/digest and loads v1 safely. Regression coverage proves FOO cannot satisfy quoted foo, exact quoted capture/revert, mixed quoted owner/name with comments/edition modifiers, unsupported ambiguous syntax skips, unquoted uppercase normalization remains stable. Fresh core lib tests (480/480), focused tests, rustfmt, and core all-target clippy -D warnings passed.

title: [QA100][P0][profiles] Switch from one immutable config generation
description:
Finding

oracle_switch_profile assembles one logical lane switch from several independent config loads separated by database awaits, then commits without rechecking drain/config generation. A concurrent config replacement can combine primary connection, stateless pool, operating policy and custom-tool signing policy from different generations.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:836-875 makes the primary and stateless connectors each call resolve_profile_options_with independently before separate connection round trips.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:5437-5463 awaits both, then profile_dispatch_policy reloads config again; main.rs:1100-1167 reloads it yet again for require_signed_tools/catalog.
- profile_dispatch_policy at dispatch/mod.rs:225-235 silently substitutes a READ_ONLY default on config failure; custom_tools_require_signatures at main.rs:1110-1116 silently substitutes false for an unprotected profile.
- Drain/exposure are checked only at dispatch/mod.rs:5418-5427 before all awaits. Commit at 5479-5510 does not compare generation or recheck drain.

Impact

A switch can pair an old high-privilege/different-database connection with a newer ADMIN policy under the same profile name, or mix primary and stateless connections from different targets. A transient reread failure can also disable an explicit require_signed_tools policy. This crosses the connection-policy safety boundary and can execute approved statements on a database/profile generation the operator did not authorize.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Each individual load is valid, but their combination is not an atomic profile identity. Campaign bead .28 covers revival of an already-drained stale lane after later reloads; it does not cover mixed-generation preparation/commit. Full tracker search found no switch-snapshot race.

Proposed fix

Resolve one immutable ResolvedProfile/config-generation snapshot containing both connection options/pool settings, level/timeout, signing/catalog and exposure policy. Build every prepared resource from it. Immediately before commit, atomically verify the generation is still current and not drained; on mismatch abort and close prepared resources while preserving the old lane. Never default a safety policy after a load failure. Share the generation model with bead .28.

Guard tests

- Block generation-1 connector after resolution, install generation 2 with a different DB and policy, release; switch rejects stale preparation and preserves the old lane.
- Primary/stateless connection labels, level, timeout and catalog always report one generation.
- Config failure cannot turn require_signed_tools=false or commit default policy.
- Drain/removal/exposure change during every await point aborts before state swap.
- Model/property test proves no committed lane mixes generations under concurrent reload/switch.
notes: Verified true by code-path reconstruction and deterministic race tests. The old switch path independently reloaded and resolved profile state across connector awaits, with no generation check at commit. Fix: ProfileDrainState now owns the accepted immutable OracleMcpConfig snapshot and monotone per-profile generations. One ProfileGenerationLease carries the exact snapshot through admission, one ProfileConnector resolves one ResolvedProfile and returns a primary/stateless ProfileConnectionBundle, policy and custom catalog derive from that lease, and commit_generation atomically refuses stale preparation before any lane swap. Split connector compatibility paths were removed. Config apply uses exact expected-snapshot CAS and rejects non-hot or authority-expanding reloads. Guards prove rotating secret refs are resolved once per bundle, both physical options match, blocked generation-1 preparation is rejected after generation-2 reload while the old lane survives, a fresh generation-2 switch succeeds, signing and timeouts stay generation-consistent, and config failure fails closed. Independent compliance verdict PASS. Gates: config 106 unit plus parity/example; core 414 plus integration/trybuild; server lib 199, bin 79 and all integrations; clippy -D warnings, fmt, diff check, and cargo deny all pass.
close_reason: Verified and fixed: profile switches now resolve and atomically commit one immutable accepted config generation; comprehensive race and guard tests pass.

title: [QA100][P1][streaming] Make emitted row and chunk SSE IDs honestly resumable
description:
Finding

Streaming row/chunk SSE frames advertise monotonic resumable IDs such as row/1 and chunk/1, but the resume parser accepts only IDs whose prefix before slash is numeric, and the replay store retains only the final JSON-RPC response. Reconnecting with an emitted frame ID is rejected as 400 and no frame replay exists.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/sse_writer.rs:46-59 emits chunk/<seq> and calls it monotonic/resumable.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:5827 onward emits row/<seq> and chunk/<seq> in live tool streams.
- stream_event_sequence/parse_stream_cursor at 5583-5601 parse id.split(/).next() as u64, so row/1 and chunk/1 are invalid.
- HttpToolStream::append_final_response at 5820-5824 stores only the final response, never row/chunk frames.
- docs/feature-rollout-0.8.0.md promises resumable chunk frames; tests assert emitted labels but never reconnect.

Impact

A dropped streaming connection cannot resume as advertised, and a standards-following client that sends Last-Event-ID from the server receives a client-error response. Large queries may need to restart, duplicate work or lose delivered-position truth.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Query cursors may offer application-level continuation, but they do not make these transport IDs resumable. Full tracker search found the closed streaming feature bead, not this contradictory resume contract.

Proposed fix

Define one ordered per-request event namespace bound to MCP session/request. Append row/chunk frames to a cardinality- and byte-bounded replay store before socket write, accept the exact emitted IDs via Last-Event-ID and specify gap/expiry behavior. Alternatively remove IDs/resumable claims until implemented and direct clients to query cursors.

Guard tests

- Disconnect after row/chunk N; GET with that exact emitted ID resumes at N+1 exactly once.
- Cross-session/request IDs are rejected.
- Buffer eviction yields typed gap/410 semantics.
- Final response ordering has no gaps/duplicates.
- Large rows obey byte caps and dropped sockets have documented DB cancellation behavior.
close_reason: Confirmed: row/chunk IDs were neither parseable nor retained. Implemented session-bound replay IDs, frame-before-write retention, exact Last-Event-ID resume, cross-session rejection, bounded gap semantics, and regression tests for row/chunk/final ordering and replay.

title: [QA100][P2][docs] Synchronize dependency provenance and decoupled release versions
description:
Finding

The v0.8.0 tag and current main pin Asupersync 0.3.5 plus OracleDB and OracleDB protocol 0.8.2, but several current operational and agent-facing documents describe older dependency combinations. The release-surface guide also states that driver and server versions must match even though v0.8.0 deliberately decoupled the trains.

Evidence

- Cargo.toml:34 pins asupersync 0.3.5; lines 46 and 55 pin oracledb and oracledb-protocol exactly to 0.8.2.
- Cargo.toml:22 and 52 still call the driver 0.7.2.
- AGENTS.md:29-33 states asupersync 0.3.4 and oracledb 0.7.1.
- README.md:220-223 and 857-860 state asupersync 0.3.4 and oracledb 0.8.0.
- docs/operations.md:23-29 states asupersync 0.3.4 and oracledb 0.5.1.
- docs/TOOLCHAIN.md:19-25 states asupersync 0.3.4 and oracledb 0.7.2.
- docs/release-surfaces.md:7-16 says exact driver pins must equal the workspace/server version and names the old pin-test pattern; scripts/release_surface_sync_check.sh correctly validates the independent driver version instead.
- ADR 0001 also reads like a current version claim; either mark its numbers explicitly as decision-time history or point to the live manifest.

Impact

Agents are instructed to read AGENTS and README before acting, so the stale contract can drive incorrect dependency diagnostics, release assumptions, and bug reports. Source builders/operators receive contradictory provenance. Future release work can mistakenly re-couple the driver and server trains, undoing an intentional v0.8.0 design decision.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Exact versions in a historical ADR may legitimately be historical; the fix should preserve that record by labeling decision-time context instead of silently rewriting history. The other surfaces explicitly describe the current branch/pin. Two independent current-state passes confirmed the manifest/tag values. Prior release-doc beads are closed and covered older upgrades; no open issue tracks the post-v0.8.0 drift.

Proposed remediation

- Update governing/current docs and Cargo comments to manifest truth.
- Rewrite release-surfaces to distinguish the nine synchronized server workspace versions from independently pinned, mutually consistent driver/protocol versions.
- In historical ADR text, mark versions as decision-time examples and link to Cargo metadata for current values.
- Reduce future drift by deriving or checking displayed dependency provenance from Cargo metadata/lockfile, or avoid hard-coded patch versions where they add no operator value.

Guard tests

- Extend release_surface_sync_check or a focused docs contract test to compare any declared current Asupersync/driver/protocol versions with cargo metadata.
- Assert driver/protocol equality with each other while permitting a server version that differs.
- Include AGENTS, README, operations, TOOLCHAIN and current Cargo comments in the checked inventory.
- Add a fixture proving server 0.8.0 plus driver 0.8.2 is accepted and a mismatched driver/protocol pair is rejected.
- Existing pinned-nightly and driver-seam gates remain green.
close_reason: Synchronize dependency-provenance / decoupled-version docs. Fixed stale version strings across docs + Cargo.toml comments: Cargo.toml (two comments oracledb 0.7.2->0.8.2), docs/TOOLCHAIN.md (asupersync 0.3.4->0.3.5, oracledb 0.7.2->0.8.2), docs/adr/0001 (asupersync 0.3.4->0.3.5), docs/operations.md (asupersync 0.3.4->0.3.5, pin example :0.7.2->:0.8.0). Fixed the semantic error in docs/release-surfaces.md: it claimed the driver pins must MATCH the workspace version, contradicting the now-decoupled release (server 0.8.0 vs driver 0.8.2 since 0.8.0) — reworded to say driver pins are independent =X.Y.Z pins on the separately-versioned driver train verified against the driver's own version, and corrected the pin-test row name to pin_is_0_8_2_and_seam_intact. Doc-only; historical CHANGELOG + the downgrade-to-0.7.2 doc left intentionally unchanged.

title: [QA100][P2][pool] Degrade to the healthy session when optional pool bootstrap fails
description:
Finding

Startup successfully opens the authoritative pinned Oracle session, then treats any failure bootstrapping the optional stateless metadata pool as failure of the entire runtime and replaces the live session with a stub. Dispatch already supports safe fallback to that pinned session when no stateless pool exists.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:911-918 opens session first, then propagates try_open_stateless_connection failure with ?.
- open_runtime_connections at 958-964 converts any combined error into stub_runtime_connections, dropping the proven primary.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:5796-5814 explicitly falls back to state.conn when stateless_conn is None and arms the read-only backstop for generated reads.
- Pool bootstrap may eagerly open min_idle connections, so connection 2/3 failure or session-limit pressure disables connection 1.

Impact

A transient pool-only failure takes every live tool offline despite a usable guarded session; recovery requires restart/profile switch instead of operating in the already-supported degraded mode.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The stateless pool is optional in SessionContext and dispatch has a deliberate safe fallback, so all-or-nothing startup is not required for correctness. Full tracker search found no pool-bootstrap degradation bug.

Proposed fix

Make the primary session authoritative. On pool bootstrap failure, emit a structured sanitized warning/metric and return RuntimeConnections { session, stateless: None }. Surface degraded readiness/capabilities without secrets. Define profile-switch behavior consistently while preserving the atomic generation safeguards in bead .58.

Guard tests

- Fake primary success + pool failure retains primary; metadata reads use the guarded fallback.
- Pool success uses stateless connection.
- Primary failure still produces stub/failure.
- Doctor/readiness reports pool degradation separately without credential/DSN leakage.
- Later recovery can install a pool without replacing/mixing profile generation.
close_reason: Already fixed in main (verified at 3290301): try_open_runtime_connections_with catches optional stateless-pool bootstrap failure in a match, logs a sanitized tracing::warn (failure_class=optional_pool_bootstrap_failed, never renders the driver error — no DSN/cred leak), and returns RuntimeConnections{session, stateless:None} instead of ?-propagating; primary-session failure stays fatal. runtime_connection_strategy() reports distinct 'hybrid_pool_degraded', wired into doctor. Guard tests present in main_tests.rs (hybrid_pool_degraded; primary_failure_remains_fatal_and_skips_optional_pool_bootstrap). Code confirmed genuinely new via git -S (introduced in commit ce4c6a7). Not force-closed: independently grep-verified.

title: [QA100][P2][protocol] Validate JSON-RPC before selecting streaming dispatch
description:
Finding

Stateful oracle_query streaming requests are recognized and dispatched before the normal JSON-RPC validator runs. The fast-path predicate checks method/id/params/name/arguments/streaming but not jsonrpc=2.0 or valid request-id semantics, so malformed protocol messages can reach Oracle when the ordinary path would return -32600 without dispatch.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:5134-5155 pattern-matches the raw serde_json Value and accepts any id value without checking jsonrpc.
- handle_mcp_post_exchange at 5261-5276 calls start_tool_stream_blocking_with_context directly when the predicate matches.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:919-945 is the normal path that requires object, method, jsonrpc exactly 2.0 and notification/id rules before dispatch.

Impact

Invalid JSON-RPC such as version 1.0 or malformed id can consume database work and produce malformed response IDs instead of the promised fail-fast protocol error. The SQL classifier still runs, so this is protocol/cost side effect rather than an authority bypass.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The optimization deliberately bypasses the shared request handler; malformed requests are reachable only on streaming=true, proving divergent semantics. Full tracker search found streaming feature work but no validation-order bead.

Proposed fix

Parse/validate one typed JSON-RPC request once, then select the streaming optimization only from a valid tools/call enum. Preserve notification behavior, valid ID types, param schema, auth and progress metadata. Route errors through the same JSON-RPC/tool-result envelope as nonstreaming.

Guard tests

- Missing/wrong jsonrpc, invalid id types, malformed params/arguments, notifications without id and batch inputs never start a stream or DB call.
- Each returns the same code/id semantics as the ordinary path.
- Valid streaming calls remain progressive and auth/scope/classifier gates execute once.
- Property test compares fast and ordinary validators for arbitrary JSON values.
close_reason: Validate JSON-RPC before selecting the streaming dispatch. handle_mcp_post_exchange selected the streaming path via streaming_oracle_query_call(&parsed) — which pattern-matched the raw Value for method==tools/call + name==oracle_query + streaming==true — BEFORE the real validator (handle_jsonrpc_request_with_context_outcome) ran, so a malformed JSON-RPC request with the streaming shape was streamed unvalidated. Fix: streaming_oracle_query_call now requires a well-formed JSON-RPC 2.0 envelope — jsonrpc==2.0 and an id that is a string or number (not missing/null/structured) — before returning Some; an invalid envelope returns None and falls through to the main dispatcher, which validates it and returns a proper JSON-RPC error. Unit test: valid streaming request selects streaming; missing/wrong jsonrpc, structured id, null id, notification (no id), and non-streaming all fall through. Existing streaming integration tests (which send valid 2.0 envelopes) unaffected. Green: core lib 582/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P2][guard] Bound transient confirmation-grant stores
description:
Finding

ExecGrantStore is an unbounded HashMap. Several agent-triggerable preview surfaces issue long-lived confirmation grants without purging expired entries or enforcing a per-lane capacity, so an authenticated client can grow a lane indefinitely by previewing and never consuming.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/exec_grant.rs:107-145 defines an unbounded map and issue always inserts.
- purge_expired exists but the only production call is oracle_preview_sql at crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:4988-4999.
- Session-level preview at 2004-2014, compile at 3561-3573, patch at 4407-4416 and create-or-replace at 4671-4684 issue without purge/cap.
- Rate limiting slows insertion but does not bound memory over a long-lived lane.

Impact

A client can create persistent grant/token state until lane close or process OOM. Multiple preview caches adjacent to grants may amplify the retained SQL/source.

False-positive and duplicate checks

TTL does not reclaim memory unless purge/consume is called. Full tracker search found the closed single-use-grant feature but no capacity defect.

Proposed fix

Centralize grant issuance behind purge plus a hard per-lane/global capacity. Evict only expired entries; if live capacity is full, return structured AT_CAPACITY/retry guidance rather than evicting usable confirmations. Expose redacted counts/metrics and audit capacity refusal. Review adjacent patch/approved-token preview maps under the same budget.

Guard tests

- Issue cap+1 through every preview surface with long TTL; length stays bounded and the last request is typed refusal.
- Expired entries are reclaimed on every issue path.
- Consume, profile switch, generation change and lane close clear as documented.
- Capacity handling never makes an old token replayable or evicts an active grant.
- Concurrent issuers cannot exceed the cap.
close_reason: Bound the transient ExecGrantStore. issue() previously inserted unconditionally with no cap, and only oracle_preview_sql called purge_expired — the other 6 issue() call sites (session-preview/compile/patch/create-or-replace/query paths) let the store grow unbounded. Moved bounding INTO issue() so every call site inherits it: on each mint, first retain(!expired) to drop expired grants (single-use + TTL-bounded), then enforce a hard cap MAX_LIVE_GRANTS=1024 by evicting the grant nearest expiry (min_by_key on deadline.remaining()) — eviction fails closed (evicted token -> Unknown on consume). 3 new tests: issue purges expired before insert; hard cap holds under MAX+50 live mints; eviction targets nearest-expiry so a long-lived keeper survives. Updated purge_drops_only_expired ordering (issue now purges on mint). Green: guard exec_grant 10/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Make execute_approved DML rollback by default
description:
Finding

The legacy execute_approved compatibility tool turns an omitted commit field into commit=true in both explicit-SQL and token-only paths. A preview confirmation authorizes the statement but silently becomes commit intent, directly violating the repository absolute invariant that DML never auto-commits and the documented rollback-by-default contract.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:2436-2446 and 2478-2487 set commit: args.commit.unwrap_or(true).
- crates/oraclemcp/src/registry.rs:916-940 advertises commit default true.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/tests.rs:4278-4300 explicitly proves a token-only DML call commits once and never rolls back.
- AGENTS.md requires never auto-commit DML. README.md:211 promises DML rollback by default and explicit confirmation before commit; docs/behavior-inventory.md:81 states the same guarded-execution contract without an alias exception.

Impact

A client following the token-only compatibility flow can permanently modify Oracle data without ever expressing commit=true. Confirmation of exact SQL is not confirmation of transaction durability, so the operation crosses a safety boundary the operator contract says is explicit.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is intentional legacy behavior in code/schema/test, but repository rules explicitly prioritize clean architecture over backward-compatibility shims and prohibit the behavior absolutely. Full tracker search found only broad closed epics, no defect bead.

Proposed fix

Default omitted commit to false for every alias and canonical path. Require explicit commit=true for durable DML and retain confirmation, durable write intent, audit and in-doubt handling. Require explicit commit for DDL/Admin as already documented. Prefer migrating/removing the compatibility-specific semantic fork so aliases share one ExecuteArgs contract.

Guard tests

- Token-only and explicit-SQL DML with omitted commit execute then rollback: commit count 0, rollback count 1, live row absent.
- Explicit commit=true commits exactly once with grant/intent/audit.
- DDL/Admin without explicit true refuses before Oracle I/O.
- execute_approved, oracle_execute and all aliases share the same default property.
- Schema/README/behavior inventory no longer advertise an exception.
notes: VERIFIED TRUE BUG. Pre-fix regression changed token-only omission to expect rollback and failed on unchanged production: actual committed=true/rolled_back=false. Root cause: both compatibility branches used args.commit.unwrap_or(true), conflating SQL confirmation with durability intent. Fixed through one commit=args.commit.unwrap_or(false); schema/docs and capabilities golden updated. Guards cover both omission paths, replay, explicit-commit concurrency, DDL pre-I/O refusal, and schema parity. Focused 5/5, registry 1/1, lib 173/173, e2e/docs 13/13, exact capabilities snapshot, clippy -D warnings, and rustfmt pass. Fresh-eyes review found the initially stale golden snapshot; corrected before commit, with no remaining logic blocker. UBS staged scan was triaged: its exit 1 is file-wide legacy/test heuristic noise (test panic/expect, inert test token literals, Instant TTL mistaken for randomness); no finding is introduced by the production two-line default change, and UBS itself reports fmt/clippy/check/build/audit/deny clean. Mock records exact DB calls, sufficient for this pure dispatch default.
close_reason: Verified auto-commit invariant violation; now rollback-by-default with regression, DDL pre-I/O refusal, schema/docs parity, and green focused/package gates.

title: [QA100][P3][http] Preserve typed native parser errors on the wire
description:
Finding

The native socket parser detects Content-Length above 1 MiB but returns generic io::ErrorKind::InvalidData; the server maps every such parser error to HTTP 400. The tested 413 path constructs HttpRequest directly and is not reachable for an actually served oversized request. Oversized headers similarly become 400 rather than 431.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/wire.rs:12-79 returns invalid_data for header limit, body limit and syntax errors alike.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:6307-6324 maps every InvalidData to status 400.
- The existing body-limit regression bypasses wire parsing by constructing HttpRequest directly.

Impact

Real clients/proxies receive the wrong status and retry/diagnostic behavior differs from tests and in-memory handler contract.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The parser safely avoids reading the oversized body, so this is not a memory bypass; it is a deterministic native-wire contract bug. Full tracker search found no 413/431 parser bead.

Proposed fix

Return a small typed parse error carrying status/class: body too large 413, headers too large 431, malformed syntax/length 400. Serialize a bounded no-store error and close before auth/dispatch.

Guard tests

- Socket-level declared body over limit returns exact 413 status/reason and does not read/allocate the body or call auth.
- Over-limit headers return 431.
- Boundary sizes pass; malformed length remains 400.
- All parser errors close the connection and cap reflected detail.
notes:
Additional native header-limit bypass

wire.rs checks buf.len() > MAX_HEADER_BYTES only after failing to find the CRLF terminator. If the terminator arrives in the same 8 KiB read that crosses 64 KiB, the loop breaks before the check and accepts an over-limit header block by nearly one chunk. The typed parser fix must compare header_end/buffer length to the limit before breaking. Add socket tests with the terminator at limit, limit+1, and the worst same-chunk overage; all over-limit cases return 431 without auth/dispatch.
close_reason: Confirmed native-wire contract bug. Added typed parser errors carried through io::Error: 413 for declared bodies over 1 MiB, 431 for header blocks over 64 KiB (including same-read terminators), and 400 for malformed syntax. Native transport now emits bounded no-store text errors with exact reason phrases and closes before auth/dispatch. Boundary sizes remain accepted. Guard evidence: wire 7/7, socket-level 413/431/400/exact-cap test green, strict core all-target clippy green.

title: [QA100][P3][alpha] Wire or remove dormant custom-tool execution modes
description:
Finding

Two documented custom-tool abstractions are production-unreachable. Large-catalog meta-dispatch exists only as library/test code while production always registers first-class tools; Form-B package calls require SideEffectOracle purity proof but the production loader always uses the default UnknownOracle at READ_ONLY, so no proof source can reach it.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/custom_tools.rs:599-702 defines oracle_run_named/register_meta_dispatch and promises a bounded large-catalog surface; repository search finds no production caller outside this module/tests.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:2103-2106 always calls register_first_class.
- Form-B proof tests at custom_tools.rs:1247-1275 inject a proven SideEffectOracle. Production load_custom_catalog_for_profile at main.rs:1119-1167 constructs Classifier::new with UnknownOracle and max READ_ONLY, with no engine/proof injection.

Impact

Large catalogs expand the MCP surface contrary to docs, and supported-looking package-call definitions fail to load. This is alpha-contract/slop debt, not a current guard bypass because Unknown fails closed.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Both paths are tested as intended features, not merely private prototypes, yet no production wiring reaches them. Full tracker search found broad closed feature work but no reachability issue.

Proposed fix

Either wire the modes end to end or remove them from the supported contract. Meta mode needs a catalog threshold/config, registration, dispatcher routing and dynamic discovery consistent with bead .45. Form B needs a production, generation-bound purity source; absent proof it must remain refused. Until then document only first-class Form-A rows as supported.

Guard tests

- Build the real production server from a catalog above threshold and assert only oracle_run_named is listed/callable with active-profile isolation.
- Load a proven package definition through the real loader and execute it; unknown proof remains fail-closed.
- Docs/schema advertise only paths covered by production reachability tests.
- Feature-off builds do not retain misleading dead APIs.
close_reason: Wire-or-remove dormant custom-tool execution modes: chose the safe SCOPE-DOWN (accepted config == behavior, fail-closed). Two surfaces were accepted-but-unreachable in production: (1) meta-dispatch (RUN_NAMED_TOOL/register_meta_dispatch/run_named/catalog_json) — production always registers via register_first_class, nothing calls the meta path (reachable only from tests) — REMOVED the dead machinery; (2) Form-B package calls (call=...) — the production loader builds Classifier::new(default UnknownOracle, no with_oracle) at a ReadOnly ceiling, so a package call fails closed to Guarded>=ReadWrite and could only ever OverCeiling (never load/execute); the SideEffectOracle proof source is the plsql-intelligence backend, out of scope offline. So Form-B is now REJECTED at load in body()/validate() with an actionable LoadError::Invalid (mirrors .44 output_mode=scalar), before classification even with a proving oracle. Removed the misleading ToolBody::PackageCall variant + its classify/dispatch arms; the call field stays (detected/reported, HMAC-authenticated). Docs already accurate. Green: core lib 602/0 (39 custom_tools), oraclemcp lib 246/0, bin main_tests 115/0 (production_loader_rejects_form_b + tools/list never advertises oracle_run_named), fmt+clippy clean. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P2][lease-alpha] Make release and expiry atomically revoke lease handles
description:
Finding

LeaseManager validates a lease under its per-lease mutex, unlocks, returns an Arc, and each operation locks it again. Concurrent release/reap/release_all can remove and roll back that Arc between validation and the second lock; the stale operation still owns the Arc and can run DB work after the lease was reported released.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/lease.rs:284-306 implements the check-unlock-return window.
- renew, commit, savepoint, preview and other operations lock the returned Arc later.
- release at 318-326 removes from the map then locks/rolls back the same Arc. reap_expired/release_all at 509-557 follow the same remove/drain then lock pattern.
- oracle_session is not currently advertised by oraclemcp, making this public-library/family alpha debt rather than a shipped remote path.

Impact

Revocation, shutdown and expiry are not linearizable: late DB work can occur after rollback/release, and the physical session stays alive through stale Arc ownership.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The async mutex serializes the two operations but cannot invalidate a previously cloned Arc. Full tracker search found no stale-handle revocation bead.

Proposed fix

Store explicit Active/Revoking/Revoked lifecycle state and generation in Lease. Mark revocation while holding the same lock used to begin an operation and recheck state/deadline inside that critical section, or return an operation guard rather than a raw Arc. Drain in-flight work before one rollback/drop and reject all stale generations.

Guard tests

- Deterministically obtain a prevalidated handle, release/reap/release_all, then every stale operation fails without mock DB calls.
- Race renew vs release and preview vs shutdown under deterministic runtime.
- Rollback occurs exactly once and connection drops after in-flight work drains.
- Expiry cannot be renewed after its revocation point.
close_reason: Confirmed stale Arc revocation race. Added lifecycle state and same-lock map/expiry validation across every lease operation; release, reap, shutdown, and uncertain preview cleanup now revoke atomically. Regression proves a prevalidated handle cannot cross release, rollback occurs once, and late DB work is rejected. lease tests 18/18; strict DB clippy and fmt pass.

title: [QA100][P2][lease-alpha] Quarantine uncertain savepoint and session-setting calls
description:
Finding

Lease savepoint, DBMS_OUTPUT enablement and allowlisted ALTER SESSION calls propagate every execute error while leaving the lease mapped/reusable. Cancellation/timeout can occur after Oracle changed state or tore the socket; savepoint can open a transaction before in_transaction is set, causing later release to skip rollback.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/lease.rs:383-396 sets in_transaction only after SAVEPOINT returns successfully.
- enable_dbms_output at 471-478 and apply_session_statement at 485-499 execute then return errors without uncertain-state handling.
- preview_dml at 410-466 already recognizes is_uncertain_session_state and removes/quarantines, proving the intended family contract.
- oracle_session is currently dormant in this binary, so severity is P2 alpha/library scope.

Impact

A torn/unknown Oracle session can be reused, carrying an open transaction or changed session state into later operations despite the closed contract that uncertain sessions are discarded.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Deterministic Oracle application errors may leave a session usable; the defect is failure to distinguish uncertain boundary errors. Full tracker search found no savepoint/session-setting quarantine bead.

Proposed fix

Centralize lease DB calls through an uncertain-error classifier. On cancellation/timeout/connection ambiguity, atomically revoke/remove the lease and attempt bounded force rollback where state may have changed; retain only a documented allowlist of proven-safe deterministic errors.

Guard tests

- Each method mock returns Cancelled after recording a DB-side effect; lease disappears, later operations fail and no session is reused.
- Rollback/discard outcome is structured and truthful.
- Proven deterministic validation/server errors remain usable only where safe.
- Live SAVEPOINT cancellation test proves no open transaction survives.
close_reason: Confirmed uncertain post-effect calls left leases reusable. Centralized same-lock quarantine/removal with best-effort rollback; savepoint and preview now conservatively mark transaction state before the ambiguous boundary; DBMS_OUTPUT and allowlisted ALTER SESSION discard only uncertain failures. Five regressions prove all uncertain paths revoke with no later DB calls and deterministic Oracle errors remain reusable. lease tests 23/23; strict DB clippy passes.

title: [QA100][P2][lease-security] Bind every lease action to its authenticated owner
description:
Finding

Lease IDs are predictable pid/counter strings. Acquisition stores a server-derived agent identity, but every later manager/router action accepts only lease_id and never checks the current principal against that owner. If oracle_session is exposed, one principal can predict and control another principal's lease.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/lease.rs:211-214 generates lease-<pid>-<counter>; acquire stores agent_identity.
- renew/release/commit/rollback/savepoint/session-setting APIs take only LeaseId.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/session_tool.rs:215-229 derives context.principal_key only for acquisition; lines 235-309 route later actions with raw IDs and no subject.
- The current oraclemcp binary intentionally does not advertise oracle_session, so this is an alpha release-gate issue, not a presently exposed remote exploit.

Impact

After future/variant exposure, cross-principal prediction permits renewing, mutating, committing, rolling back or releasing another client's pinned Oracle session. Error differences may also enumerate live leases.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Stamping agent identity into Oracle is audit metadata, not authorization. Full tracker search found no owner-check/unguessable-handle bead.

Proposed fix

Use cryptographically random or MAC-bound opaque handles and require the validated server subject on every manager operation. Compare owner before revealing existence/expiry, preferably constant-time where relevant, and return an indistinguishable denial. Keep caller-supplied identity absent from schemas.

Guard tests

- Two principals: every cross-owner action fails before DB calls; same owner succeeds.
- Sequential/predictable IDs are absent and collision behavior is safe.
- Unknown and wrong-owner errors do not form an enumeration oracle.
- Refreshed same-principal tokens retain access under the canonical OAuth tuple; different clients do not.
close_reason: Confirmed predictable unbound lease handles. Replaced pid/counter IDs with collision-safe 256-bit random opaque handles; every public lease action now requires and uniformly verifies the canonical server owner under the operation lock before expiry/state/DB access; release is authenticated and fallible; router rejects missing subjects. Guard tests cover all cross-owner actions with zero DB calls, same-owner refreshed access, unknown/wrong indistinguishable denial, and random uniqueness/format. Full DB suite passed (377 unit plus integration/compile-fail/doc tests); session router 11/11; strict DB+core Clippy and public API snapshot pass.

title: [QA100][P3][dashboard] Make rename preview syntax-aware or label it text-only
description:
Finding

Workbench labels boundary-delimited lowercase substring matches as identifier usages and builds a rename preview by replacing them all, without PL/SQL lexical/AST awareness or replacement validation. It renames inside comments/string/q-quoted literals and mishandles quoted identifiers/case semantics.

Evidence

- web/src/app/App.tsx:7460-7489 lowercases the whole source and searches substrings using only a character-boundary check.
- buildRefactorPreview at 7492-7512 substitutes unchecked replacement text at every match.
- UI at 6537-6585 labels the count usages and the result rename preview even though the same Workbench already obtains parser definition spans.

Impact

The preview-only feature can present a dangerously false refactor artifact that an operator copies into the governed editor, including invalid or unintended source changes. It does not directly execute, so P3.

False-positive and duplicate checks

A text replacement tool would be valid if labeled honestly; the defect is semantic terminology/presentation and lack of lexical exclusion. Full tracker search found no rename-preview bead.

Proposed fix

Prefer PL/SQL analysis-engine reference spans plus Oracle identifier validation and scope awareness. Until available, explicitly label it textual find/replace, exclude comments/literals with a lexer, validate replacement syntax and never call matches semantic usages.

Guard tests

- Comments, ordinary/q-quoted strings and identifier substrings are not semantic replacements.
- Quoted/mixed-case identifiers, Unicode/multibyte offsets, local shadowing and qualified references follow parser spans.
- Invalid replacement is rejected.
- Edits apply in reverse/exact spans without offset drift.
close_reason: Confirmed misleading semantic-rename preview. Replaced substring search with a lexical Oracle-identifier token scan: comments, block comments, ordinary/national strings, q/nq-quoted literals, and differently quoted identifiers are excluded; unquoted matching is case-insensitive and quoted matching exact. Find/replacement syntax is validated, exact JS selection offsets are preserved, and UI now truthfully labels text matches/text preview with a scope-awareness disclaimer. Focused 18/18, full dashboard 28/28, and tsc --noEmit green.

title: [QA100][P2][changelog] Reconstruct the real v0.8.0 release history
description:
Finding

CHANGELOG.md gives v0.8.0 the wrong release date and driver version, then reduces a 127-commit, 175-file release to two bullets. It does not let an operator or future agent understand what materially shipped.

Evidence

- CHANGELOG.md:9 dates 0.8.0 as 2026-07-07; tag 40371d1 and the GitHub release were published on 2026-07-09.
- CHANGELOG.md:11 says the server adopted oracledb 0.8.0; the tagged Cargo.toml pins oracledb and oracledb-protocol 0.8.2.
- git history for v0.7.2..v0.8.0 contains 127 nontrivial commits across 175 files, with 26,281 insertions and 2,298 deletions.
- Material shipped waves omitted from the entry include OCI IAM token sources, TNS alias and timeout/keepalive support, SQL guard tightening, audit chain v4 fingerprinting, flashback reads, DDL dependent previews, optimizer cost/cardinality, streaming query SSE, dashboard/operator surfaces, Kani/mutation/metamorphic/golden discipline, and release hardening.
- The GitHub release links only the full comparison, so the durable changelog is the expected orientation layer.

Impact

Operators cannot assess upgrade risk or discover new opt-ins from the canonical history. Future agents must re-mine 127 commits and may misattribute regressions or repeat delivered work. The wrong driver/date facts also contaminate release diagnostics and downstream notes.

False-positive and duplicate checks

A changelog should synthesize notable capabilities rather than list every commit, but two bullets omit multiple operator-visible and security-critical changes and include two false facts. The dedicated changelog quality bar requires correct date/version, capability-wave grouping, representative links, and tracker integration for this repository size. Existing release workstream beads are closed and no bead tracks changelog reconstruction.

Proposed remediation

Research only the bounded v0.7.2..v0.8.0 window from git history, tag/release metadata, closed release workstreams, upgrade/rollout docs, and shipped artifacts. Rewrite the 0.8.0 entry into concise Added, Changed, Fixed, Security and Validation or equivalent capability waves. Use the actual 2026-07-09 date, driver 0.8.2 provenance, a live release link, representative commit links, and precise bead/workstream references. Keep it an orientation layer, not a raw commit dump or marketing page.

Guard and acceptance checks

- Version/date and driver assertions match tag metadata and Cargo metadata.
- Every major shipped workstream is represented or explicitly judged non-notable in a small research coverage ledger.
- Links distinguish the GitHub Release from raw tags and resolve to representative commits/workstreams.
- The entry names security-impacting fixes and operator-visible migration/opt-in behavior.
- A changelog validation script or release checklist checks structural headings, release link, concrete date, and dependency provenance for the new entry.
- No historical entry is rewritten without evidence from its own tag window.
close_reason: Reconstruct the real v0.8.0 release history in CHANGELOG. Entry claimed date 2026-07-07 and 'oracledb 0.8.0'; the real tag is 2026-07-09 (git tag) and the pin is =0.8.2 (Cargo.toml). Rewrote the [0.8.0] entry in Keep-a-Changelog form grounded in the actual v0.7.2..v0.8.0 commits: Changed = adopted oracledb 0.8.2 (via 0.8.1) + asupersync 0.3.5, wired K1/K2 accessors; Added = K10 streaming query SSE, K9 flashback AS-OF, K2 capability probe; Security = SEC-1 oracle_query_execute re-classify+re-gate at apply; Breaking = ErrorEnvelope/GuardDecision structured-reason fields. Doc-only.

title: [QA100][P1][exports] Bind materialized data to the originating principal
description:
Finding

Materialized query-export access is bound only to the sorted OAuth scope strings, not the authenticated principal/session/lane. The export registry is server-wide, so a second principal with the same scopes can read the first principal's export if it learns the oracle-export URI.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/export.rs:76-119 defines ExportAccess and token_fields solely from scope_fingerprint; profile is explicitly advisory.
- read at 235-265 verifies the MAC and stored entry against that same scope string only.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:1632-1639 derives export access from scope_grant and discards the available DispatchContext principal.
- Scopes lower operating level but do not identify a tenant/client. Two users commonly share oracle:read.

Impact

Cross-principal disclosure of potentially sensitive Oracle result data is possible through URI leakage in logs/chat/browser history or guessing/capture. The MAC prevents forging IDs but authenticates the wrong boundary.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The code comments call scopes the cross-tenant boundary, but OAuth scopes are permissions, not subjects. Closed E3 acceptance promised access identical to the originating query. Full tracker search found no principal-binding follow-up.

Proposed fix

Include the validated server principal key at minimum in ExportAccess and every signed/stored comparison; preferably also bind session/lane and reproducible profile generation according to intended resume semantics. Retain the scope ceiling. Return indistinguishable not-found/denied and never expose binding material.

Guard tests

- Principal A mints sensitive export; B with identical scopes cannot resources/read it, while A can.
- Refreshed token for the same canonical principal succeeds only if intended.
- Different scope/profile/session cases follow documented policy.
- Cover OAuth, client credentials and mTLS identities plus URI leakage attempts.
- MAC vectors include every access field.
close_reason: Confirmed same-scope principal B could read principal A's export. Bound export MAC and stored access to canonical principal plus exact scopes, preserved same-principal refresh/session/lane/profile resume, canonicalized anonymous HTTP vs stdio, unified denial envelopes, and added registry/server/dispatch/auth/golden regressions. Focused core/oraclemcp/clippy gates green.

title: [QA100][P1][config] Bind Apply to the exact reviewed preview draft
description:
Finding

Dashboard Config Apply can run without a preview and is not bound to the bytes/diff that were previewed. After previewing harmless draft A, an operator can edit to security-sensitive B while A's diff remains visible, then Apply sends B with only the current-file base SHA; the server re-stages and writes B.

Evidence

- web/src/app/App.tsx:1731-1755 stores a preview but Apply sends the current mutable draftToml and only activePreview.current_sha256 (or status SHA).
- onChange at 1803-1808 does not clear preview. canSubmit at 1783 and both buttons at 1811-1829 require only nonempty text/session, so Apply works before Preview.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:408-438 re-stages whatever draft arrives and compares expected only to current/base SHA, not preview.draft_sha256 or diff identity.

Impact

An operator may believe the displayed redacted diff is the change being applied while different ceilings, auth, connection refs or exposure settings are written. This defeats the review gate at a safety-critical configuration boundary.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The server validates B syntactically and atomically; the defect is missing review-to-apply authorization binding. Closed W2 specifies Draft -> validate -> redacted diff -> atomic write. Full tracker search found no follow-up.

Proposed fix

Stage should return an opaque single-use TTL preview token bound to target path, base SHA, exact draft SHA, redacted-diff digest and operator/session. Apply must supply token plus matching bytes/hash and reject drift, replay, expiry or current-base change. UI clears preview on every edit, disables Apply until editor SHA matches preview draft SHA, and requires deliberate confirmation for ceiling/auth/drain/restart changes.

Guard tests

- Preview A then edit/apply B is rejected with no write; direct dashboard apply without preview is rejected.
- Current file race, cross-session token, replay and expiry all fail.
- Exact A applies and audit links preview/apply digests without raw secrets.
- Any textarea edit immediately hides/invalidates the old diff and Apply state.
- Security-relevant changes require the documented confirmation step.
close_reason: Fresh verification reproduced unreviewed apply and preview/draft substitution. Bound operator config apply to bounded five-minute single-use preview authorities scoped to exact operator/browser session, base SHA, draft SHA and redacted-diff digest; added sensitive-change confirmation, replay/expiry/race fail-closed handling, secret-safe review evidence, and immediate UI invalidation on edit. Focused 3 service regressions plus served HTTP workflow pass; TypeScript check passes.

title: [QA100][P2][dashboard] Confirm credential rotation and revocation deliberately
description:
Finding

Client credential Rotate and Revoke execute immediately on one button click. Rotation invalidates the bearer, closes sessions and returns the replacement only once; revoke terminates access. The same dashboard explicitly confirms lane cancellation, but these higher-consequence controls have no confirmation or typed identity.

Evidence

- web/src/app/App.tsx:2024-2053 mutations call destructive endpoints directly.
- Row buttons at 2262-2279 invoke them on click with no confirm/modal.
- Lane cancellation at 615-644 documents and implements an accidental-click confirmation.
- CSRF/action tickets stop cross-site requests but do not prevent operator misclicks or reusable-ticket double submission.

Impact

A stray click can revoke production access or rotate a secret before the operator is ready to capture/distribute its one-time replacement, closing active sessions.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is deliberate human-safety UX, not server authorization. Full tracker search found no rotate/revoke confirmation bead; retry/idempotency is tracked separately.

Proposed fix

Use a modal that names client, generation, scopes and session-closure impact. Require typed client ID or a strong second step for revoke. Rotation must warn that the bearer is shown once and keep it visible/copyable until explicit acknowledgement. Deduplicate/disable while pending.

Guard tests

- Cancel does no request; exact selected client alone is mutated.
- Double-click sends once.
- Replacement bearer remains available until explicit acknowledgement and never reappears later.
- Timeout/error never renders success.
- Keyboard/focus/accessibility behavior prevents accidental default confirmation.
close_reason: Confirmed one-click destructive credential UX. Rotate/revoke now open an accessible Escape-cancellable dialog naming exact client, label, generation, scopes, active-session closure, and irreversible/one-time consequences; exact case-sensitive client ID is required, pending state deduplicates submission, and cancellation sends nothing. Rotation clears prior success before dispatch and retains the one-time bearer in a selectable/copyable panel until explicit saved acknowledgement. Errors cannot render a stale success. tsc green; focused 19/19 and full dashboard 29/29 green.

title: [QA100][P2][IAM] Prove the selected endpoint is TCPS before resolving a token
description:
Finding

The pre-I/O IAM token gate treats a wallet/cert setting or any protocol=tcps substring as proof the selected Oracle endpoint is TLS. A plaintext EZConnect/PROTOCOL=TCP target with a wallet passes, and a mixed descriptor whose selected first address is TCP passes because a later address says TCPS. Token file/env/exec is then consulted despite the explicit before-I/O refusal contract.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/iam_token.rs:430-448 returns true for compact.contains(protocol=tcps) or wallet_location/cert-DN presence.
- inject_iam_token_with at 476-493 relies on it before executing/reading the token source.
- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs:1026-1044 duplicates the inference.
- A wallet/cert config supplies credentials/trust material; it does not rewrite host:1521/service or PROTOCOL=TCP into TCPS. The pinned driver later reparses and rejects AccessTokenRequiresTcps, preventing proven wire exfiltration but only after secret retrieval.

Impact

Plaintext/mixed configuration can execute token_exec or read secrets unnecessarily and relies on downstream defense for the actual socket. Commands may have side effects and short-lived token material is exposed to more process memory than promised.

False-positive and duplicate checks

No wire leak was demonstrated because the driver rejects the selected plaintext endpoint; severity is P2 defense-in-depth/side-effect failure. Full tracker search found closed IAM delivery work, no selected-endpoint defect.

Proposed fix

Use the authoritative connect-string/TNS alias parser and resolved address selection model. Require the actual selected address protocol to be TCPS; never infer transport from wallet/cert presence alone. Share one resolver with the driver and retain downstream rejection.

Guard tests

- Plaintext + wallet + token_exec marker refuses before the command runs.
- TCP-first mixed descriptor never resolves a source; TCPS-first/selected succeeds.
- TNS alias resolution checks its selected address, including fallback rules.
- Missing/malformed alias fails before token I/O.
- Driver AccessTokenRequiresTcps remains defense in depth.
close_reason: Verified real: wallet/cert material and later PROTOCOL=TCPS substrings could trigger token env/file/exec resolution before the driver rejected a plaintext selected endpoint. Fixed by sharing one alias-resolving oracledb-protocol first-address transport proof between pre-token injection and the driver adapter. Added wallet+TCP no-spawn, TCP/TCPS mixed-order, TNS alias, missing/cyclic alias, downstream defense, full db/core tests.

title: [QA100][P1][http] Route progress and list-changed notifications per session
description:
Finding

Server-initiated progress and tools/list_changed notifications are drained only by the stdio loop. HTTP sends the JSON-RPC response/result stream but never drains the shared NotificationHub, even though initialize advertises tools.listChanged=true. Notifications accumulate/drop; a naive global drain would let concurrent lanes steal each other's events.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:577-591 drains resource and server notifications only after stdio frames. Repository search finds no HTTP production call to drain_server_notifications.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:5260-5340 serializes responses/SSE/result-store events without notification-hub delivery.
- server.rs:1794-1816 unconditionally advertises tools.listChanged true; profile switch enqueues it. Progress notifications share the same global hub.

Impact

HTTP clients never receive advertised catalog changes or progress; custom-tool/profile discovery goes stale. Global queue capacity can be consumed by undeliverable events, and later wiring without ownership would cross-deliver progress tokens/profile changes between principals.

False-positive and duplicate checks

HTTP SSE itself works for responses, but no code bridges this notification queue. Full tracker search found feature work, no missing-transport defect.

Proposed fix

Make notification queues lane/session/request-stream scoped. Route progress to the originating response stream and list_changed to the owning session(s), with bounded replay/cursors consistent with streaming beads. Stateless JSON mode must define a compliant delivery channel or advertise listChanged=false/suppress unsupported notifications.

Guard tests

- Concurrent stateful HTTP A/B calls with distinct progress tokens receive only their own start/finish events.
- A profile switch delivers list_changed to A only, followed by refreshed discovery.
- No cross-drain; disconnect/replay/gap behavior is deterministic and byte-bounded.
- Stateless and stdio capability advertisement matches actual delivery.
- Queue saturation is observable without leaking tokens/principals.
close_reason: Fresh verification confirmed the global stdio-only drain and absent HTTP bridge. Replaced it with a globally bounded request-owner queue plus persistent session catalog tracking; stateful HTTP now emits and replay-retains progress/list_changed on the exact originating SSE stream (including row streaming), concurrent owners cannot cross-drain, saturation exposes only a redacted drop count, all session-close paths clear state, and stateless JSON advertises listChanged=false. Added owner isolation, replay, capability, cleanup, profile refresh, streaming, and conformance guards; core 615/615, oraclemcp 247/247, strict clippy green.

title: [QA100][P1][config] Make redacted previews complete without exposing values
description:
Finding

Config redacted diff snapshots reduce security-sensitive sets to counts and paths/refs to presence booleans, while profiles are reduced to public ProfileMetadata. Same-cardinality/presence substitutions therefore produce no diff entry and consequential profile changes may render zero changes.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/config_ops.rs:499-526 stores only host/origin/issuer/scope/operator-subject counts and booleans for OAuth/TLS/audit shipping/key presence.
- redacted_snapshot at 528-565 uses config.list_profiles ProfileMetadata, omitting connect string, username, credential/login/trusted-session/OCI/DRCP/proxy/app-context details.
- Replacing allowed origin A with B, OAuth issuer, operator subject, cert/key/CA path, SIEM/WORM target or secret reference while preserving count/presence makes the snapshots equal for that field.
- Tests prove non-leakage but not semantic completeness.

Impact

An exact reviewed draft can change authentication, network trust, audit destination, database identity or session policy while the UI displays no corresponding redacted change. Combined with the Apply workflow, this undermines operator review.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Raw secret/topology values must remain hidden, but hiding values does not require hiding that a semantic field changed. This is distinct from bead .71, which binds apply to a preview; this bead makes that preview truthful. Full tracker search found no completeness follow-up.

Proposed fix

Define allowlist-first field-by-field change descriptors that always report added/removed/changed. For sensitive values expose type/presence plus a domain-separated digest/stable opaque revision; for sets expose added/removed counts and hashes rather than cardinality only. Group complex profile changes under explicit opaque categories when granular disclosure is unsafe.

Guard tests

- Property/invariant: any unequal validated configs produce descriptors covering every changed semantic field or documented opaque group.
- Same-count host/origin/issuer/scope/subject substitutions register.
- Credential/connect/session/OCI/TLS/audit destination swaps register without raw values.
- No host, DB/user, path, token/key ref, context literal or secret leaks.
- Diff output is deterministic and binds into bead .71 preview token.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed: config previews now emit deterministic process-local HMAC opaque revisions for complete config/HTTP/audit/profile semantics plus field/set descriptors; same-cardinality trust, identity, TLS, audit, credential, routing, session, and context changes are visible without raw values. Added real stage-path non-leak/completeness/determinism regressions; 15/15 focused tests and 530/531 full core (only concurrent QA schema-diff partial failed).

title: [QA100][P3][IAM-alpha] Make token refresh arithmetic saturating and nonnegative
description:
Finding

The public IAM token refresh seam adds signed now_unix + skew_secs without validation. Debug builds panic at i64 overflow, release builds wrap, and negative skew can make an already-expired token appear reusable; ensure_fresh_token then returns it.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/oci.rs:260-271 implements is_expired but needs_refresh uses unchecked signed addition.
- ensure_fresh_token at 287-301 reuses current solely when needs_refresh is false.
- Example now=1100, exp=1000, skew=-200 computes 900<1000 and reuses expired material.
- No production IamTokenSource ships, so this is library/alpha rather than current runtime exposure.

Impact

Embedders can panic or reuse expired credentials at time extremes/misconfiguration.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Negative skew has no useful refresh meaning and expiry should dominate every proactive window. Full tracker search found no arithmetic bead.

Proposed fix

Accept Duration/u64 or reject/clamp negative skew, use saturating_add and always check is_expired(now) before the proactive threshold. Return typed configuration error if preserving signed input.

Guard tests

- i64::MAX boundary never panics/wraps and refreshes.
- Expired token plus negative skew is never reused.
- Exact expiry/skew boundaries and ordinary positive skew retain intended behavior.
- Property test proves once expired, increasing now never returns to fresh.
close_reason: Make IAM/OCI token-refresh arithmetic saturating and nonnegative. IamToken::needs_refresh did unchecked signed now_unix + skew_secs >= expires: a negative/misconfigured skew made an already-expired token read as fresh (ensure_fresh_token then reused it), and the add could overflow i64. Fix: unconditional is_expired(now_unix) pre-check (an expired token ALWAYS needs refresh regardless of skew sign), then a nonnegative-clamped, saturating proactive-window check (skew.max(0), now.saturating_add(skew)). 3 new guard tests: expired-under-negative-skew always refreshes, saturation at i64::MAX no overflow, ensure_fresh_token never reuses an expired token under a hostile negative skew. Green: oraclemcp-db oci tests 14/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P3][subscriptions-alpha] Bind subscribers and pending updates to transport identity
description:
Finding

The dormant resource-subscription API trusts caller-supplied clientId and queues only bare changed URIs in one global drain. Subscriber identities are not used for routing, so one caller can unsubscribe another and whichever transport drains first would consume updates for everyone.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:1177-1213 handles subscribe/unsubscribe without DispatchContext and accepts parsed client.
- subscribe_params at 1760-1782 reads caller clientId/_meta.clientId or defaults every connection to client.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/subscriptions.rs:212-258 records changed URIs but pending carries no subscribers; drain_pending globally removes them.
- The thin binary currently keeps the change source unsupported, so this is alpha/protocol debt, not a shipped exposed path.

Impact

If enabled as designed, subscription ownership and multi-client delivery are broken, enabling cross-client cancellation, lost notifications and cross-delivery.

False-positive and duplicate checks

ClientId may be useful metadata but cannot be an authorization/routing identity. Full tracker search found only the closed generic subscription feature.

Proposed fix

Keep the API explicitly unavailable/remove dormant claims, or bind subscriptions to server-derived session+principal and maintain per-owner pending queues/transport routing. Disconnect cleans only that owner. Do not expose subscriber identity.

Guard tests

- A/B subscribe same URI with forged identical clientId; B cannot cancel A.
- One change yields exactly one update per owning stream with no cross-drain.
- Disconnect/reconnect cleanup and bounded replay are per session.
- Unsupported builds advertise/return no subscription capability.
close_reason: Bind subscribers + pending updates to transport identity. resources/subscribe|unsubscribe keyed off a client-supplied clientId (subscribe_params read params.clientId/_meta.clientId — untrusted → owner spoofing), and SubscriptionHub.pending was one global VecDeque drained with no per-subscriber filter (whichever transport drained first consumed everyone's updates). Fix: subscribe_params -> subscribe_uri (parses only the URI; clientId ignored for routing/authz); new subscription_owner(context) derives the owner from the transport principal (DispatchContext::principal_key(), fallback STDIO_SUBSCRIPTION_OWNER) — the JSON-RPC dispatch site already has context and now threads it into handle_resource_subscribe/unsubscribe. pending is now HashMap<owner, VecDeque<uri>>; on change, enqueue_updates fans each changed URI to registry.subscribers_of(uri) appending per-owner (lock order registry->pending, no ABBA); drain_pending(owner)/drain_resource_updated_notifications(owner) return only the caller's queue (stdio loop drains the stdio owner); unsubscribe_all(owner) drops that owner's pending. Preserved supports-subscriptions/method-not-found + stdio single-client behavior. 5 new tests (owner-spoof rejected; per-principal drain isolation; two-principal forged-clientId end-to-end; unsubscribe_all scoping). Necessary call-site update in mcp_conformance.rs (drain signature). Green on main: subscriptions 12/0, core lib 602/0, conformance subscribe 2/0, fmt+clippy clean. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P0][stdio-auth] Require successful initialize before any protected request
description:
Finding

Required stdio init-token authentication is enforced only inside initialize. The JSON-RPC router permits tools/call, resources/read/list, prompts and completion before initialize, so a client can skip initialization/token presentation and directly dispatch database or write tools.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:539-593 passes StdioAuthPolicy for every frame and resets lifecycle state.
- handle_jsonrpc_request_with_context_outcome at 919-988 routes every method unconditionally; only the initialize arm calls handle_initialize.
- handle_initialize at 991-1034 validates the token and sets stdio_negotiated. That field otherwise prevents only a second initialize; it is not an authorization gate.
- Existing auth tests exercise missing/wrong token on initialize, not tools/call as the first frame.

Impact

When stdio auth is configured Required, an untrusted local child/client can invoke all served MCP tools without knowing the secret by omitting initialize. Profile ceilings/classifier still apply, but the entire transport authentication boundary is bypassed, including permitted guarded writes under configured profiles.

False-positive and duplicate checks

MCP lifecycle requires initialize before ordinary operations, and the project explicitly treats the init token as authentication. Full tracker search found no pre-initialize bypass issue.

Proposed fix

Add one lifecycle/auth gate before method dispatch: under Required, reject every operation except a valid first initialize (and only explicitly protocol-allowed pre-init messages) until token validation and negotiation succeed. Failed initialize must leave state unauthenticated; initialize state transition must be atomic with successful validation. Keep Disabled behavior explicit.

Guard tests

- Spy dispatcher: tools/call and resources/read/list before initialize or after wrong initialize never reach it.
- Correct token initialize unlocks allowed calls; second initialize remains refused.
- Notifications/ping behavior follows the chosen MCP lifecycle exactly.
- Binary pipe E2E sends tools/call as first frame with token configured and proves refusal/no DB I/O.
- Fuzz/model test covers arbitrary pre-init method sequences.
notes: VERIFIED TRUE SECURITY BUG. Pre-fix control flow and regression proved Required stdio auth validated only initialize while tools/call and other protected methods routed before token presentation; a first-frame binary tools/call reached normal dispatch. Fixed with one centralized pre-dispatch lifecycle/auth gate. Fresh-eyes review caught the MCP-required pre-init ping exception and missing ping handler; official 2025-11-25 lifecycle permits ping and requires empty result, now implemented/tested. Failed initialize never unlocks; correct initialize unlocks; second initialize rejects; notifications are one-way and cannot unlock; HTTP and Disabled stdio are unchanged. Regression matrix covers every routed protected method, unknown extension, ping request/notification throughout lifecycle, exact response count, dispatcher count, and child-process first-frame tools/call. Gates: focused 2/2, binary E2E 1/1, core lib 412/412, conformance 24/24, core E2E 7/7, served stdio E2E 9/9, clippy both packages all-targets -D warnings, fmt/diff-check. Independent compliance review PASS. UBS file scan exit 1 was triaged as whole-file legacy/test heuristic noise (test expect/panic, JSON equality misread as secret compare, fixed test command); UBS reports fmt/clippy/check/build/audit/deny clean and no new production finding.
close_reason: Verified pre-initialize stdio authentication bypass; all protected routes now fail closed until valid initialize while spec-required ping remains available, with unit, conformance, and real-binary guards.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Classify sequence NEXTVAL as non-read-only
description:
Finding

The SQL guard has no NEXTVAL pseudocolumn handling. UDF detection looks for identifier followed by an opening parenthesis, so SELECT app_seq.NEXTVAL FROM dual follows the UDF-free plain-SELECT Safe/READ_ONLY path and oracle_query admits it before Oracle. NEXTVAL mutates sequence state independently of commit/rollback.

Evidence

- Repository-wide guard search finds no NEXTVAL/CURRVAL rule. user_defined_calls in crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs recognizes call syntax, not dotted pseudocolumns.
- Plain SELECT becomes Safe when calls is empty/proven in classify_statement, so sequence.nextval is not surfaced as a side effect.
- Oracle SQL Reference documents that NEXTVAL increments and returns a sequence value; CREATE SEQUENCE documentation states generation increments independently of transaction commit or rollback, and session sequences are accessible even on read-only databases.

Impact

A tool promised to admit only proven read-only SQL can irreversibly advance database/application sequence state. Transaction rollback and the read-only backstop cannot restore it, violating the central fail-closed invariant.

False-positive and duplicate checks

CURRVAL is observational after session initialization; NEXTVAL is explicitly mutating. Full tracker search found sequence health work but no classifier defect.

Proposed fix

Add Oracle-token/AST-aware sequence pseudocolumn detection. Refuse .NEXTVAL on oracle_query/read-only paths; if supported through oracle_execute, classify at least READ_WRITE with explicit warning that rollback does not undo it and require the ordinary preview/confirmation/audit path. Keep .CURRVAL semantics distinct.

Guard tests

- Bare/schema/quoted sequence NEXTVAL in every legal SELECT position refuses read-only before DB I/O.
- Whitespace/comments/case/DB-link forms cannot evade detection.
- CURRVAL policy is explicit. A genuine quoted column named nextval is conservatively handled/documented.
- Live isolated sequence: oracle_query refusal leaves value unchanged; governed path advances exactly once with audit and non-rollback warning.
- Metamorphic quote/comment/parenthesis variants never lower classification.
notes: Verified true. Pre-fix SELECT app_seq.NEXTVAL classified Safe/READ_ONLY and reached the read path. Token-aware detection now marks sequence NEXTVAL as a permanent READ_WRITE effect while preserving aggregate DDL/ADMIN severity and distinguishing CURRVAL, comments/literals, and quoted columns. Fresh review caught and fixed two deeper issues: rollback-default NEXTVAL originally bypassed confirmation, and execute-with-rowcount does not fetch SELECT rows, so query-shaped NEXTVAL could falsely report success. The final design refuses query-shaped NEXTVAL before grant consumption or DB I/O and supports only governed DML/PLSQL with an exact-SQL single-use confirmation, durable intent, rollback permanence warning, and quarantine on uncertain outcome. Live Oracle proved execute without fetch does not advance, while DML and PL/SQL advance exactly once even when row changes roll back. Tests: guard 118 plus integration/property/metamorphic suites; core query-execute regression; six server NEXTVAL regressions; registry contract regression; public API baseline exact. Focused clippy passed. UBS staged shadow check/clippy/test-no-run/audit/deny passed; heuristic findings were existing test panic/unwrap inventories and false-positive token/randomness patterns, with no actionable changed-line defect. Independent compliance rereview PASS.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed: NEXTVAL is fail-closed on read paths, query-shaped effects are refused without fetch, and governed permanent effects require exact confirmation plus durable intent.

title: [QA100][P2][release] Align rollback runbook with the current tag pipeline and channels
description:
Finding

The governing release flow and rollback runbook describe the old multi-workflow and npm-era topology rather than the v0.8.0 pipeline that actually shipped.

Evidence

- AGENTS.md:72-78 routes Docker publication to docker.yml and MCP publication to publish-mcp.yml as though those are normal tag workflows.
- docs/release-checklist.md:130-132 says release.yml, docker.yml and publish-mcp.yml all build from a pushed tag.
- In the live workflow files, release.yml is the tag pipeline and publishes binaries, crates, GHCR and MCP registry; docker.yml and publish-mcp.yml are manual workflow_dispatch auxiliaries.
- docs/release-checklist.md:142-178 is still titled for broken v0.6.0, hard-codes that version in actions, and requires npm rollback even though README explicitly states that no npm/npx channel is offered.
- scripts/e2e/release_rollback_dry_run.sh:1-26 defaults the broken release to 0.6.0 and previous to 0.4.1; lines 75-83 always plan auxiliary Docker/MCP dispatches and npm deprecation/dist-tag mutations.
- The current release includes signed multi-platform artifacts, GHCR, MCP registry and pending Homebrew/winget channels, which need current incident semantics.

Impact

An operator following the canonical emergency runbook can target obsolete versions, attempt mutations against a nonexistent npm package, misunderstand which workflow is authoritative, and spend incident time dispatching auxiliary workflows without reconciling the tag pipeline. The governing AGENTS contract also sends future release agents down the wrong path.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Manual docker.yml and publish-mcp.yml remain useful for recovery/variants, but they are not the ordinary tag publishers. A historical v0.6.0 incident appendix could be preserved as history, but it is presented as the active rollback runbook. The actual v0.8.0 workflow run and current YAML prove the topology. No open bead tracks this post-release operational drift.

Proposed remediation

- Document release.yml as the single normal tag pipeline and clearly label docker.yml/publish-mcp.yml as manual recovery or variant tools.
- Replace version defaults with required explicit broken and previous-good inputs, or derive/review them from release metadata; never silently target years-old versions.
- Generate the crate list from workspace metadata and the channel plan from the current release contract.
- Remove npm actions while npm is not a supported channel; include them only behind an explicitly detected/configured channel.
- Make rollback actions conditional on what was actually published, keep public deletions/yanks separately approval-gated, and record signed artifact/GHCR/MCP/Homebrew/winget state.
- Preserve an archival v0.6.0 incident note separately if historically valuable.

Guard tests

- A dry run for a synthetic current version names only currently supported/published channels.
- No default or implicit outward-facing version is accepted.
- Workflow topology test asserts tag publication is owned by release.yml and auxiliary workflows are labeled manual.
- Adding/removing a release channel forces the rollback coverage fixture to update.
- npm commands are absent while README/release config says no npm channel.
- Dry-run remains non-mutating and emits an exact, reviewable plan; every irreversible command remains operator-gated.
close_reason: Verified against the successful v0.8.0 tag run and current workflows. release.yml is the sole normal tag publisher; docker.yml/publish-mcp.yml are dispatch-only auxiliaries. Replaced implicit 0.6.0/0.4.1 and npm-era plan with required explicit versions, Cargo-metadata crate discovery, publication checks, conditional approval tiers, current signed/GHCR/pending-registry channel inventory, and truthful immutable MCP Registry incident semantics. Added focused topology/channel/non-mutation regression coverage; targeted Rust test and strict clippy plus release dry gates are green.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Forbid transaction control inside caller PL/SQL
description:
Finding

Balanced PL/SQL containing COMMIT or ROLLBACK is classified only READ_WRITE. With oracle_execute commit omitted or false, confirmation is not consumed; the block can commit internally, then the server rollback succeeds as a no-op and the response/audit falsely report RolledBack.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:141-152 side-effect markers omit transaction control.
- classify_statement at 1237-1240 treats Commit/Rollback/Savepoint as Guarded READ_WRITE; benign PL/SQL body folding at 1443-1545 retains that level.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:3178-3189 consumes confirmation only when args.commit is true.
- execute_sql_inner at 3255-3357 executes the block, then calls connection rollback for commit=false and records RolledBack solely from that cleanup call.
- A block that updates a row and commits internally therefore persists with no confirmation while the outer rollback cannot undo it.

Impact

This bypasses both rollback-by-default and explicit-confirm-before-commit, permanently changing data while returning/auditing a rollback. It directly violates the core invariant.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Oracle permits transaction control in appropriate PL/SQL contexts and an outer rollback cannot reverse an earlier commit. Full tracker search found no nested transaction-control bead.

Proposed fix
