Fail closed on COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT and SET TRANSACTION anywhere inside caller-controlled PL/SQL. Even explicit commit=true should not allow hidden transaction boundaries because the server cannot own/audit their exact outcome; use ordinary server-controlled transaction execution instead. Cover package-mediated/dynamic controls with the opaque-call fix.

Guard tests

- A commit=false/no-token block with inner update+commit refuses before DB I/O and live row remains unchanged.
- Nested IF/LOOP/exception, comments/case, COMMIT WRITE, ROLLBACK TO, SAVEPOINT and SET TRANSACTION cannot evade.
- Mock modeling an inner commit proves post-exec rollback can never be labeled RolledBack for admitted text.
- Explicit commit=true does not authorize hidden inner boundaries.
- Metamorphic wrapping never loses transaction-control detection.
notes: VERIFIED TRUE. Fresh source trace confirmed both nested and standalone caller COMMIT reach execute_sql_inner at READ_WRITE, require no confirmation when commit=false, then can make the outer rollback a no-op while audit reports RolledBack. Fix adds a tokenizer-based invariant before the operator allow-list: caller COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT, and SET TRANSACTION are always Forbidden, including mixed case, comments, nested IF/LOOP/exception blocks, COMMIT WRITE and ROLLBACK TO. Literals, comments, quoted identifiers, and longer identifiers are excluded. Internal server commit/rollback methods and the direct SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY backstop do not traverse the caller classifier and remain intact. A machine-stable TRANSACTION_CONTROL reason and safe rewrite tell callers to submit only DML and let oracle_execute own the boundary. Dispatch regression proves commit=false and commit=true variants are refused before execute, commit, or rollback I/O. Exact allow-list cannot bypass the invariant. Gates: guard 121 unit plus all adversarial/metamorphic/property suites; server lib 200; error 16; affected all-target clippy -D warnings; release classifier baseline 20,168 ns/statement under the 100,000 ns ceiling; fmt/diff; cargo deny green with existing unmatched-allowance warnings only.
close_reason: Restore verified completion after stale worker reassignment; implementation and compliance evidence remain in f160a1f.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Fresh compliance PASS: all transaction-control syntax families are caught from the bare token stream, data-only mentions remain non-matches, final Classifier enforcement precedes allow-listing, and no server-owned transaction primitive is routed through this guard. No blocker.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Claim released without source edits because the parent reassigned this agent to the direct rust-oracledb campaign.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Fail closed on opaque PL/SQL package calls
description:
Finding

Balanced anonymous PL/SQL defaults to READ_WRITE unless it contains one of nine text markers. Opaque package/procedure calls that the parser cannot semantically classify contribute no higher floor. A READ_WRITE-capped profile can therefore invoke packages that perform DDL/Admin effects, bypassing the DDL ceiling and step-up.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:141-152 uses a short marker denylist. DBMS_UTILITY is absent.
- Benign balanced blocks at 1443-1545 floor at Guarded/READ_WRITE; block interior parsing only raises recognized segments and unparseable calls contribute nothing.
- A block invoking DBMS_UTILITY.EXEC_DDL_STATEMENT with destructive DDL has no listed marker and is leveled READ_WRITE. String contents are intentionally hidden from marker matching.
- Oracle DBMS_UTILITY documentation states EXEC_DDL_STATEMENT executes its string as DDL. Many other packages can have effects, making a denylist non-exhaustive.

Impact

A profile whose max_level is READ_WRITE can execute DDL or other higher-authority package effects using its database grants without DDL/Admin confirmation, violating the operating ladder ceiling.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The database still checks caller privileges, but max_level intentionally constrains use even when the credential has broader grants. This is distinct from stored CREATE leveling fixes. Full tracker search found no opaque-call issue.

Proposed fix

Treat every caller PL/SQL routine/package invocation as opaque unless a semantic SideEffectOracle proves its effect level. Conservatively classify unknown calls at Admin/Forbidden or at least no lower than DDL where provable, with a tiny reviewed safe allowlist only if necessary. Replace marker-denylist authority with an allow/proof model.

Guard tests

- A DBMS_UTILITY destructive-DDL block classifies DDL-or-higher/Forbidden and a READ_WRITE live fixture preserves its target with zero DB call.
- Case/comments/concatenated string/package synonym/CALL syntax variants cannot evade.
- Other unproven SYS and application package calls fail closed.
- Proven-safe allowlist entries remain narrowly scoped and cannot accept dynamic SQL.
- Property test: wrapping an opaque call never lowers its required level.
notes: Verified true security bug and implemented fail-closed containment. Reproduced the pre-fix READ_WRITE classification of DBMS_UTILITY.EXEC_DDL_STATEMENT. Caller DECLARE and every non-reviewed BEGIN body now refuse before Stage A allow-listing; the only engine-free anonymous subset is comment-insensitive NULL plus exact unquoted SYS.DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE with literal/bind-derived inert arguments. All explicit CALL forms refuse even under an all-accepting purity oracle because the current two-field ObjectRef cannot bind Oracle runtime identity exactly. Qualified PlsqlSideEffectOracle lookup no longer falls back to an unrelated bare node. Static DML guidance, registry schemas, capability golden, custom-tool fixtures, metamorphic and generated wrapping invariants, zero-DB-I/O dispatch coverage, and the live ladder were updated. FREE 23ai live run passed twice: DBMS_UTILITY returned FORBIDDEN_STATEMENT and the target remained present; audit restart/verification passed. Independent fresh-eyes completion review: PASS. Gates: fmt and diff clean; workspace Clippy all targets with warnings denied; cargo test workspace exit 0; cargo deny clean apart from configured unused allowances; full guard adversarial/property/metamorphic suites; 201 server tests; feature-gated qualified-purity test; Python syntax; UBS findings triaged as pre-existing/test-only. Follow-ups discovered and filed: .101 valid package specs, .102 zero-argument SQL functions, .103 catalog-aware CALL identity.
close_reason: Fail-closed PL/SQL containment implemented and independently verified with unit, property, zero-I/O, feature, full-workspace, and live FREE 23ai target-preservation evidence.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Do not admit unproven view and policy side effects as read-only
description:
Finding

The served raw-query guard uses the default UnknownOracle and leaves statement_unknown_guarded disabled. A UDF-free SELECT from any table/view is therefore Safe even when its hidden view dependency or VPD/policy function performs autonomous committed writes. The engine-backed semantic classifier exists but is not bound to served dispatch.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:579-589 constructs DEFAULT_CLASSIFIER with Classifier::new(UnknownOracle); ensure_read_only at 1358-1368 uses it.
- crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:1147-1185 explicitly keeps statement-level Unknown permissive unless with_statement_unknown_guarded is enabled.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/plsql_tools.rs:242-250 builds the real SideEffectOracle with Unknown tightening, but no served dispatch caller uses it. Its test at 1407-1433 explicitly proves the default calls SELECT FROM APP.ORDERS Safe while an engine model tightens hidden trigger effects.
- Oracle documents autonomous functions invoked from SQL can insert+commit data and remain committed after the caller rolls back. View/VPD dependencies can hide the call from submitted text; read-only transaction backstop cannot roll back an independent autonomous transaction.

Impact

A query tool advertised as proven read-only can cause permanent definer-rights side effects through a selectable view or policy, violating the engine-free safe-by-default headline and central fail-closed invariant.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The permissive baseline is intentional for usability, but it conflicts with the stated proof contract and Oracle semantics. Closed bead .5.30 binds the engine classifier only to PL/SQL-intelligence analysis paths and even describes this gap; it is not wired to the served raw-query gate. This is therefore a real unresolved product-safety decision, not a duplicate.

Proposed fix

Make the served gate obtain semantic statement purity before admission. Options: resolve view/routine/VPD dependencies dynamically and require ProvenReadOnly; use a separately constrained Oracle credential that provably cannot invoke side-effecting definer objects; or fail closed on unproven views/policies and document the limitation. Never call Unknown proven. Cache proof by object DDL/generation with invalidation.

Guard tests

- Isolated autonomous logging function hidden behind a view/policy: oracle_query refuses before evaluation and log table remains unchanged.
- Plain table SELECT under a proven constrained path remains allowed.
- Missing/stale/incomplete catalog is Unknown and refuses.
- Engine and engine-free modes expose honest capabilities/diagnostics.
- Object replacement invalidates proof before next query.
notes: CONTAINMENT MECHANISM delivered (this session): Classifier::served_strict() / ClassifierConfig statement_unknown_guarded fails closed on every read whose base objects are not proven read-only. It is OPT-IN and deliberately NOT wired into the served DEFAULT_CLASSIFIER (under UnknownOracle it would refuse ALL reads). So .82 is STILL FAIL-OPEN BY DEFAULT on the served path. The real fix is resolver-backed statement_purity -> RES-4. Leave OPEN.
close_reason: Served read proof now refuses views and enabled SELECT VPD policies unless hidden effects are provably absent. Live autonomous view and policy witnesses remained at zero on XE18, XE21, and FREE23; dedicated unit mocks also prove caller SQL never executes.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Enforce the ALTER SESSION allowlist on raw execution
description:
Finding

Non-allowlisted ALTER SESSION statements fall through classifier parsing as generic READ_WRITE. oracle_execute never applies the hardened session-setting allowlist, so a READ_WRITE session can bypass the dedicated session tool and change container, tracing/events or other security/diagnostic state below the intended DDL/Admin ceiling.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/enforcement.rs:26-48 explicitly documents ALTER SESSION SET parse failure becomes READ_WRITE and is_allowed_alter_session is not consulted by classify.
- The allowlist at enforcement.rs:58 onward is enforced only by the dedicated session router. Its tests at 224-259 identify CONTAINER, SQL_TRACE, EVENTS and smuggled multi-assignments as forbidden.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs execute_sql_inner classifies/gates raw SQL but has no is_allowed_alter_session check.

Impact

An agent can cross the configured PDB/profile boundary with SET CONTAINER where grants allow it, alter audit/trace/event behavior or hidden session policy using only READ_WRITE elevation, bypassing the supported allowlisted path and operating ladder.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Oracle still checks database privilege, but the profile max_level is intended to constrain use of broader credentials. The code comments acknowledge the bypass as current behavior, while the threat model claims smuggling prevention. Full tracker search found only the closed dedicated-tool allowlist bead, not raw execution.

Proposed fix

Route every raw ALTER SESSION form through the same strict parser/allowlist, or classify non-allowlisted forms Admin/Forbidden and expose safe settings only through the dedicated tool. Ensure aliases/custom/PLSQL package-mediated paths cannot bypass. Prefer one parsed session-setting policy shared by classifier and executor.

Guard tests

- READ_WRITE raw CONTAINER, SQL_TRACE, EVENTS, hidden/private params, multi-assignment and comment-wedge variants refuse before DB I/O.
- CURRENT_SCHEMA/NLS settings follow the explicitly chosen safe-tool/level policy.
- Raw aliases and PL/SQL wrappers cannot bypass.
- DDL/Admin step-up does not silently enable forbidden session security changes unless an explicit policy says so.
- Live fixture confirms container/session identity never changes on refusal.
notes: Fresh verification reproduced a real P0: raw preview and execute trust Guarded/READ_WRITE classification and never consult the ALTER SESSION allowlist; commit=false needs no confirmation even though session state persists across rollback. Implementing one shared classifier boundary plus zero-I/O/live guards; independent review pending.
close_reason: Route raw ALTER SESSION through the shared alter_session_policy: non-allowlisted/malformed forms are Forbidden before the operator allow-list (Guarded+non-transactional for allowlisted), so raw oracle_execute can no longer smuggle CONTAINER/SQL_TRACE/EVENTS/hidden-params under READ_WRITE. Guard+enforcement+dispatch tests + adversarial-corpus entries green.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Post-landing verification: the full pinned workspace Clippy gate found one strict question_mark lint in the new comment scanner. Rewriting the equivalent let-else to ? preserves fail-closed behavior and makes cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings green. The complete workspace test suite was green on the landed QA83/QA84/QA102 tree. Free23 live matrix passed the QA83 container-identity scenario twice: FORBIDDEN_STATEMENT at READ_WRITE, no execution grant, CON_NAME stayed FREEPDB1; disposable principal cleanup verified.

title: [QA100][P0][guard] Never default parsed-but-unmatched DDL to READ_WRITE
description:
Finding

classify_statement maps only a subset of parsed DDL AST variants. Every other recognized statement falls through the catch-all to Guarded/READ_WRITE. OracleDialect parses COMMENT ON, ANALYZE, CREATE SEQUENCE and many ALTER/CREATE/DROP variants, so they can execute under a READ_WRITE ceiling and commit implicitly even when commit=false.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-guard/src/classifier.rs:1217-1222 handles only CreateTable/View/Index, AlterTable, generic Drop and Truncate as DDL.
- Lines 1237-1242 then map transaction control and every remaining AST variant to guarded_rw.
- sqlparser 0.62 OracleDialect supports COMMENT ON and AST variants include other schema/object DDL. Representative COMMENT ON and CREATE SEQUENCE parse successfully but have no matching DDL arm.
- Oracle documentation states DDL issues implicit commit before and after and cannot be rolled back.

Impact

READ_WRITE profiles can perform schema changes that should require DDL or ADMIN step-up. The server may then call rollback and report RolledBack even though Oracle already committed the DDL, bypassing ceiling, confirmation and outcome truth.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is broader than opaque package calls and inner transaction control: the statements parse normally but the classifier default is too low. Full tracker search found prior individual DDL fixes, no exhaustive-floor defect.

Proposed fix

Make the Oracle Statement match exhaustive for every supported DDL/DCL/Admin variant with correct DDL/Admin floors. Independently apply a conservative tokenized leading-verb/object floor so parser evolution cannot lower authority; an unknown recognized statement must be Forbidden/Admin, never READ_WRITE by default. Audit sqlparser upgrades against the corpus.

Guard tests

- Table-driven Oracle SQL Reference corpus covers COMMENT/ANALYZE and every CREATE/ALTER/DROP schema-object, policy, user, role, database and operator variant.
- Property: successful parsing can never lower the conservative leading-verb floor.
- Live representative statements under max READ_WRITE refuse before DB and preserve metadata.
- commit=false can never report RolledBack for any admitted implicit-commit statement.
- Parser-version inventory fails CI when a new Statement variant lacks policy.
close_reason: Completed exhaustive DDL/Admin floors after fresh compliance correction: profile/schema/database/PDB/security-policy operations now require Admin; zero-I/O and Free23 metadata-preservation regressions pass.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Reopened: Fresh post-commit compliance review found incomplete Admin floors: CREATE/DROP PROFILE still fall to DDL; CREATE SCHEMA is mapped DDL despite permitting embedded GRANT; DROP DATABASE and CREATE/ALTER/DROP PLUGGABLE DATABASE fall under generic object DDL although they require SYSDBA/SYSOPER and can delete database files. Reopening to add canonical Admin prefixes and regression/zero-I/O coverage.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Post-landing compliance correction completed. Fresh review found CREATE/DROP PROFILE, CREATE SCHEMA, DROP DATABASE, and CREATE/ALTER/DROP PLUGGABLE DATABASE still below Admin. Oracle's SQL reference confirms profiles control user password/resource policy; CREATE SCHEMA may embed GRANT; DROP DATABASE and DROP PLUGGABLE DATABASE require administrative privileges and can delete data/control files. Canonical comment-aware Admin prefixes now cover these plus audit/lockdown policy forms; parsed CreateSchema is explicitly Admin. Table tests assert exact floors, dispatcher tests prove representative parsed and parser-rejected DDL/Admin forms refuse at READ_WRITE with zero execute/commit/rollback calls. Free23 live matrix passed twice: COMMENT ON returned OPERATING_LEVEL_TOO_LOW and USER_TAB_COMMENTS stayed NULL before/after. Full guard suite (138 unit + integration/property), server lib 206, workspace all-target Clippy -D warnings, fmt/diff, Python syntax, and cargo deny all pass.

title: [QA100][P1][timeouts] Propagate the total request budget into DB operations
description:
Finding

Dispatch computes a whole-request RequestBudget but never installs it on the long-lived lane Cx used by DB checkpoints. with_call_timeout only checks the local budget before and after the entire body while setting the same full timeout on each round trip. Multi-roundtrip tools can therefore run N times the advertised total timeout and perform effects after deadline before returning Timeout.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:571-575 derives RequestBudget from timeout and cx.budget but returns it as a local value.
- with_call_timeout at 1105-1150 enforces before/after and sets a per-roundtrip connection timeout; it does not derive a budgeted child Cx.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/lane.rs:1141-1202 reuses one lane Cx for every command and passes it into dispatcher.
- RequestBudget docs promise DB checkpoints consume the whole-request deadline/poll quota, but those checkpoints see the unrelated lane Cx. cleanup() has no production caller. oracle_db_health runs many subchecks inside one wrapper.

Impact

Long multi-call reads exceed latency bounds; mutations may commit after the alleged deadline and then return a retryable timeout, creating duplicate/retry ambiguity. Poll/cost quota is ineffective.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Per-roundtrip call timeout is real but is not a total request budget. Closed B1/B6 beads claimed this propagation; source shows the acceptance is incomplete. No open defect bead exists.

Proposed fix

Derive a per-command child Cx carrying the effective Budget, or pass one absolute remaining deadline into every DB operation/checkpoint. Use a separate short bounded cleanup/finalizer context. Never return a plain retryable timeout after a committed/uncertain mutation; audit/quarantine the terminal outcome.

Guard tests

- Two 60ms mock operations under 100ms total cancel before operation 2/completion.
- Health multi-subcheck wall time stays within one ceiling; poll quota is consumed.
- Commit crossing deadline yields audited succeeded/in-doubt state, never ambiguous generic Timeout.
- Cleanup still runs under its own bounded fresh budget.
- Consecutive lane commands receive independent budgets.
notes: Verified as a real whole-request-budget defect and fixed across core lane orchestration, DB request limits, pool identity propagation, and served transport wiring. Absolute request deadlines and shared quotas now cover admission, factory preparation, mailbox wait, dispatcher work, and every DB round trip; per-tool timeout only tightens the profile ceiling. Caller cancellation retains the same in-flight future through a fresh bounded terminal finalizer, never first-polls an unstarted future, and durably audits UnknownDiscarded before outward finalization timeout. Prepared generation leases are bound atomically and released outside locks; same-key first calls share capacity; pre-cancelled calls allocate nothing. Cleanup uses fresh bounded limits and timeout/partial restore uncertainty quarantines. Regression coverage includes two 60ms wire calls under one 100ms ceiling, shared health quotas, late committed/audited ordinary and streaming results, ready-result-vs-spent-finalizer quota, pre-poll cancellation, factory rebuild, generation/lock/capacity paths, stdio lane use, schema/golden/API contracts. Fresh independent architecture audit found no remaining QA85 blocker. Final gates: workspace clippy -D warnings green; full workspace tests green including 444 core, 311 DB, both trybuild proofs, integrations, goldens, docs; fmt, diff-check, API lock, and cargo deny green. Separate pre-existing issues remain in beads .105-.112.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed: one absolute, quota-sharing request budget now reaches admission, lanes, dispatcher and DB wire calls with cancellation-safe terminal finalization and regression coverage.

title: [QA100][P1][dashboard-auth] Authenticate the listener before releasing a pairing secret
description:
Finding

oraclemcp dashboard --url treats any 2xx /healthz responder as the intended service, then sends it the raw one-time pairing token. Tickets are shared-directory records not bound to host/port/listener instance, so a malicious local stub can receive the token and redeem it against the real loopback listener within TTL.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/dashboard_auth.rs:291-338 probes only caller base URL health and any 2xx; tests at 648-653 explicitly accept a generic stub.
- mint at 340-389 stores token hash/purpose/time and builds base_url/dashboard/pair?ticket=raw; no listener audience/identity.
- exchange_ticket at 184-225 validates only shared-dir token file/hash/expiry and receives no listener identity.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:3075-3158 uses successful generic probe as the sole precondition to mint/open. Browser launcher also exposes the raw URL in process argv.

Impact

A same-user malicious local process that occupies/proxies the selected port can obtain a full dashboard cookie/session/CSRF/action tickets from the victim listener and invoke operator APIs. This defeats the pairing boundary explicitly intended to protect against hostile local processes/web pages.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Loopback exchange does not help because the attacker is local and can send its own request. 0600/0700 protects other users but not a same-user process that learns the raw token. Closed probe-before-mint UX accepted a stub; full tracker has no authenticated-instance follow-up.

Proposed fix

Establish a same-user authenticated instance identity/challenge in the private runtime dir. Authenticate /healthz/bootstrap and bind minted ticket metadata/exchange to exact listener instance plus scheme/host/port audience. Restrict CLI URL to loopback absent separate authenticated remote mode. Avoid placing the bearer in launcher argv, e.g. authenticated local IPC/browser bootstrap.

Guard tests

- Generic 2xx stub cannot cause mint/open or learn a usable token.
- Token for listener A fails on B even with shared runtime dir.
- Spoof/proxy, scheme/host/port drift and replay fail; intended instance pairs once.
- Same-user local-process adversarial E2E covers redemption at the real listener.
- Browser launch process metadata contains no raw pairing bearer.
close_reason: Authenticate listener before releasing pairing secret: same-user authenticated instance identity + listener-audience-bound tickets (scheme/host/port), loopback-only CLI URL, no bearer in launcher argv; dashboard_auth+main+http hardened, 5 new guard tests. Golden SSE event-id diagnosed as legitimately session-scoped resumable (sha256(session-id)[..32], CSPRNG-fresh per session, stable within stream) — normalized volatile binding in golden framework (keeps <seq> prefix), NOT blessed as random; streamable golden made production-faithful via result_store. All green: golden 6/6 x3 stable, core lib 550/0, oraclemcp lib 246/0, cli 27/0, clippy+fmt clean.

title: [QA100][P2][operator-auth] Enforce browser origin checks for ambient mTLS principals
description:
Finding

/operator/v1 skips all dashboard Origin/Sec-Fetch/CSRF/action-ticket validation whenever transport authentication produced a principal. That is reasonable for explicit bearer headers but unsafe for browser-ambient mTLS certificates. Any loopback Origin passes the generic guard, and mutation routes accept text/plain JSON, allowing a cross-origin simple request to operate as an allowlisted mTLS principal.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:1728-1755 passes only principal_key.is_some into enforce_dashboard_operator_auth.
- enforce_dashboard_operator_auth at 1915-1945 returns None immediately for any authenticated principal, bypassing exact-origin, Fetch Metadata, CSRF and action ticket.
- crates/oraclemcp-auth/src/http_guard.rs:121-129 accepts every loopback Origin regardless of Host/port.
- Exact scheme+authority validation exists at http/mod.rs:1989-1997 but only for paired dashboard sessions. Operator edge checks response Accept, not mutation Content-Type.

Impact

A malicious page/local web service can trigger config apply/rollback, credential rotation/revoke or lane cancellation when the browser presents an installed operator-allowlisted client certificate.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Non-ambient OAuth/client-credential bearer headers are not the issue. Exploitability requires a browser-usable mTLS cert mapped to operator authority, so P2. Full tracker search found no ambient-credential browser distinction.

Proposed fix

Whenever Origin or browser Fetch Metadata is present, enforce exact same-origin or a narrowly configured UI origin regardless of auth mechanism and require CSRF/action intent for ambient credentials. Alternatively reject browser-originated operator requests authenticated only by mTLS unless paired. Require application/json on mutation routes as defense in depth.

Guard tests

- Allowlisted mTLS + cross-port loopback Origin + text/plain mutation refuses with no state change.
- Same-origin paired dashboard remains valid.
- Non-browser mTLS client without Origin remains valid per policy.
- Sec-Fetch-Site cross-site fails even with mTLS; bearer behavior stays documented.
close_reason: Enforce browser origin checks for ambient mTLS principals. enforce_dashboard_operator_auth did 'if has_authenticated_principal { return None; }' BEFORE any Origin/Sec-Fetch check, so an authenticated principal — including an ambient mTLS client cert the browser auto-attaches — bypassed the CSRF defense, letting a hostile page drive the dashboard cross-origin. Fix: run the check-if-present browser-origin/Sec-Fetch defense (enforce_dashboard_get_headers) for EVERY request BEFORE the authenticated-principal shortcut. This rejects a cross-origin request even when authenticated, while non-browser clients (bearer/mTLS API callers, CLI) send no Origin/Sec-Fetch and pass unaffected. The unauthenticated browser POST pairing flow keeps its stricter require-Origin + CSRF-ticket check. Chose the check-if-present (get_headers) semantics, not require-Origin, precisely so non-browser bearer/mTLS clients aren't broken. Unit test: authenticated + cross-origin => 403; authenticated + same-origin => allowed; authenticated + no browser headers => allowed. Green: core dashboard tests 16/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P1][migration] Keep manual-review DDL inert in exported scripts
description:
Finding

Schema-diff steps labeled manual_review/executable=false embed raw target DDL after two comment lines, and render_migration_script appends the entire block verbatim. The downloaded .sql therefore contains active SQL for a step the UI says is review-only. Executable PL/SQL DDL also lacks the standalone slash delimiter needed by common Oracle script runners.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/schema_diff.rs:164-195 constructs ManualReview ddl as comments followed by raw target DDL.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/schema_diff_export.rs:247-280 special-cases ManualReview by appending that string verbatim. terminated_statement at 283-289 treats PL/SQL ending semicolon as complete without a slash.
- web/src/app/App.tsx:5727-5739 exports the artifact as .sql; step table at 5818-5850 labels it review/non-executable.

Impact

Running the advertised migration script can execute an unreviewed non-executable step or fail/combine at PL/SQL boundaries after earlier Oracle DDL already committed, leaving a partial migration contrary to the UI gate.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The header calls it a review artifact, but .sql/download and active statements make it runnable in practice. Full tracker search found the closed export feature, no inertness defect.

Proposed fix

Comment every line of manual-review payload or move it into a separate non-executing .review.md section with an explicit ALTER placeholder. If .sql is supported, render delimiters by parsed Oracle statement/object type and validate through the declared SQLcl/SQL*Plus runner; otherwise use a non-SQL extension and no active statements. Clear preview on source/title edits.

Guard tests

- Split/execute exported artifact proves no ManualReview payload is runnable, including multiline/q-quoted DDL.
- Executable PL/SQL steps receive correct slash delimiters; SQL steps terminate once.
- Supported runner parses the complete artifact without combining steps.
- Proposal statements exclude manual steps and stale UI preview cannot export/draft.
close_reason: Confirmed review-only target DDL was active in downloaded .sql and PL/SQL blocks lacked runner terminators. Manual-review DDL is now inert at migration-plan creation and defensively line-commented at export across LF, CRLF, bare CR, q-quotes, slash and SQL*Plus directives; executable package/type/routine/trigger steps receive exactly one standalone slash while plain DDL terminates once. Proposal drafting still excludes manual steps. Dashboard previews are bound to exact title/before/after inputs, including in-flight edit races. Five DB schema tests, four core export tests, 24 web tests, TypeScript, strict core clippy, rustfmt and diff checks pass.

title: [QA100][P1][query] Enforce result byte caps on the first oversized row
description:
Finding

Query pagination always includes the first serialized row even when it exceeds max_result_bytes. Nested cursor serialization has the same exception, and total_bytes omits columns/wrapper overhead. A one-row query can exceed the advertised hard 10/25 MiB response budget by orders of magnitude.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/query.rs:242-260 checks the cap only when out_rows is nonempty.
- Test byte_cap_truncates_mid_page at 453-463 explicitly locks in an oversized first row under a 10-byte cap.
- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/serialize.rs:482-501 repeats the first-row bypass for nested cursors.
- Per-cell caps allow many large columns and Oracle permits wide select lists; registry/dispatch describe max_result_bytes as a maximum/hard cap.

Impact

Authenticated queries can allocate/serialize/send responses far beyond configured bounds, causing memory/transport DoS and amplifying replay-store pressure.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Including one row ensures cursor progress but contradicts the hard byte contract; progress can be represented by a typed oversized-row skip/reference. Full tracker search found no first-row defect.

Proposed fix

Enforce a checked encoded whole-response budget including columns/metadata/wrapper. If row one cannot fit, return a bounded typed row-too-large/resource-link outcome and an explicit forward-progress cursor; never include the bytes. Use checked/saturating arithmetic and one shared budget for nested data.

Guard tests

- First row above cap yields an actual serde_json response within cap.
- Many wide columns/LOBs/nested cursors, exact boundary and wrapper overhead are covered.
- Pagination advances without repeating/skipping ambiguously on an oversized row.
- Retained/replayed response also respects global byte budgets.
- Existing ordinary pages remain byte-identical.
notes: Completion scope verified: emitted compact row-object payload now fails closed on an oversized first row, nested cursors return a bounded typed sentinel, aggregate JSON retention drops on budget crossing, and registry/rustdoc describe the exact row-payload law. The original finding also exposed three broader layers not solved by a DB payload cap: pre-accounting Vec<OracleRow>/single-cell materialization is tracked by .115; full metadata/MCP-envelope accounting is tracked by .116; stateful replay retention already exists as .52. These remain open rather than being silently claimed complete.
close_reason: Confirmed and fixed the first-row bypass itself: top-level pages return a bounded QueryRowTooLarge error without rows/cursor/replay entry, nested results return a bounded sentinel, checked budget accounting is exact, and ordinary pages remain byte-identical. Full pinned workspace gates pass; broader materialization/envelope/replay work is explicitly tracked in .115/.116/.52.

title: [QA100][P1][IAM] Enforce token_exec deadline across descendant pipes
description:
Finding

The OCI IAM token_exec runner can wait forever after its direct child exits successfully if a descendant inherited stdout or stderr. The advertised five-second wall deadline only bounds child.wait_timeout; after an early successful exit, the code unconditionally joins both pipe-reader threads. EOF does not arrive until every descendant closes the inherited write end.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/iam_token.rs:314-389 spawns two reader threads, waits only for the direct child, and then joins both readers without a deadline on the success path.
- The timeout path at lines 354-372 explicitly recognizes that inherited descendant pipe ends can keep readers blocked, but the same condition is assumed impossible at lines 383-389 after a successful direct-child exit.
- A fresh process-level reproduction used a helper that spawned sleep 10 with inherited pipes, printed a token, and exited 0. The direct child exited immediately, but draining its stdout was still blocked when an outer two-second deadline fired with exit 124.
- Existing tests cover a directly hanging sleep process and finite oversized output, but no helper that exits after daemonizing or spawning a pipe-inheriting descendant.
- No existing bead matches token_exec descendant, inherited-pipe, process-group, or join liveness.

Impact

A configured token helper can wedge database connection or reconnection indefinitely despite the documented five-second guarantee. On timeout and wait-error paths, killing only the direct child and detaching blocked readers also permits descendants and reader threads to accumulate across retries. This is a liveness and resource-exhaustion defect on a security-sensitive credential path.

False-positive checks

The command is intentionally direct argv with no shell, which prevents shell injection but does not prevent a legitimate executable from spawning children. Process exit closes only the direct process descriptors; descendants inherit them by default. The source itself already accounts for this behavior on one control-flow branch.

Proposed fix

Run the helper in a dedicated process group or platform job, terminate and reap the entire tree on every deadline/error, and collect capped stdout and stderr through deadline-aware channels instead of unbounded thread joins. Apply one end-to-end deadline to wait plus output collection. Keep errors and logs token-free.

Guard tests

1. A helper exits 0 after spawning a long-lived descendant that inherits stdout; resolution returns or fails within the configured deadline and leaves no descendant.
2. Repeat for inherited stderr.
3. A direct timeout cleans up the descendant tree and reader workers across repeated attempts.
4. Finite valid output still succeeds; oversized/nonzero/error paths remain fail-closed and redacted.
5. Assert elapsed wall time for every branch is bounded with a platform-tolerant margin.
close_reason: Verified with a fresh process-level reproducer (direct parent exited 0 while descendant-held stdout blocked beyond 12s). Fixed with cross-platform process containment (POSIX group / Windows Job Object), whole-tree termination and direct-child reap on every path, one end-to-end deadline for execution plus stdout/stderr collection, redacted failures, and 10x inherited-pipe/timeout cleanup regression stress. Focused 29/29 IAM tests, clippy, fmt, deny, and locked metadata pass.

title: [QA100][P1][client-auth] Make credential mutations transactional across memory and disk
description:
Finding

Client credential issue/rotate/revoke mutate the live in-memory ClientCredentialFile before durable persistence and do not restore it when persistence fails. Rotation can invalidate the old bearer without returning the new one; revoke can reject live auth after returning failure, then restart resurrects the disk state.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/client_credentials.rs:287-326 pushes/sorts a new record before persist_locked.
- rotate at 389-419 overwrites hash/salt/generation and constructs the sole new bearer before persistence.
- revoke at 422-443 mutates revoked/generation before persistence.
- HTTP closes principal sessions only after Ok, so a failed live mutation may alter new authentication while leaving existing lanes active.

Impact

I/O/fsync/rename failure creates split-brain authentication: lost replacement secrets, unexpected lockout/ghost credentials, failed revocation in response but live denial, or revocation resurrection after restart.

False-positive and duplicate checks

The persistence helper may be atomic on success; the defect is mutation ordering on error/ambiguous durability. Campaign bead .11 concerns per-request last-use persistence cost, not lifecycle atomicity. Full tracker search found no duplicate.

Proposed fix

Clone/prepare the next file and bearer, persist it atomically, then swap live state under lock; or restore exact prior state on every failure. Define a structured uncertain outcome if post-rename directory fsync fails and reconcile memory/disk before accepting auth. Session cleanup must follow the authoritative generation.

Guard tests

- Fault inject serialize/write/fsync/rename/dir-fsync for issue/rotate/revoke.
- Response, in-memory auth, disk file, session closure and restart always agree.
- No generated bearer is lost after becoming authoritative.
- Failed revoke never reports success or later resurrects unexpectedly.
- Concurrent authenticate/mutate observes one generation atomically.
close_reason: Confirmed issue, last-use, rotate, and revoke published live state before durable persistence. Every mutation now builds an isolated candidate, persists first, and swaps one whole generation under the store mutex. Definite failures preserve old memory/disk/auth and never close sessions; visible post-write errors reconcile exact bytes, return the one-time bearer with explicit durability evidence, and close the authoritative generation; irreconcilable state poisons all auth/mutation fail closed until restart. Fault, reopen, HTTP lifecycle, redaction, and concurrent auth/rotate guards pass; core 521/521, binary 244/244, CLI 27/27, and strict clippy pass.

title: [QA100][P1][cancellation] Make operator lane kill invalidate the MCP session
description:
Finding

Operator lane cancel closes/removes only the StatefulLaneDispatch entry. It leaves the owning HTTP session and replay buffer valid, so the next POST with the same mcp-session-id passes validation and resolve_lane spawns a fresh Oracle lane; old results remain replayable.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:2663-2714 resolves a lane binding and calls lifecycle.close_session_with_reason only. It never removes session_store/result_store.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/lane.rs:871-885 removes just the lane key.
- handle_mcp_post_exchange at http/mod.rs:5192-5266 validates the still-live session then dispatches; resolve_lane at lane.rs:763-830 creates a lane when absent.
- Lane close docs explicitly say the caller must remove the HTTP session first, but operator cancel does not.

Impact

The advertised kill switch terminates one connection/grant set only momentarily. A misbehaving client can immediately resume without initialize and historical buffered data survives the supposed termination.

False-positive and duplicate checks

This is distinct from bead .21, which covers the dashboard lacking an action ticket for the button. Full tracker search found no lifecycle invalidation defect.

Proposed fix

Atomically tombstone/remove session ownership and replay state before/with lane close, generation-bound to the resolved binding so a race cannot kill/revive the wrong lane. Return terminal status requiring a fresh initialize; coordinate in-flight requests and credential cleanup.

Guard tests

- Cancel then same-session POST/GET/DELETE is rejected and no lane respawns.
- Replay buffer is cleared; fresh initialize can create a new distinct session.
- Dispatch-vs-cancel barriers cannot continue/recreate the old generation.
- Retry is idempotent already-terminated.
- Audit records target session/lane generation and close outcome without raw principal.
close_reason: Make operator lane kill invalidate the MCP session. handle_operator_lane_cancel_route called only lifecycle.close_session_with_reason, leaving the HTTP session_store entry and the result_store stream replay buffer intact — so after an operator 'kill' the MCP session id stayed resolvable and its buffered results remained replayable. Now mirrors handle_mcp_delete's teardown in the lane.rs-required order: session_store.remove(mcp_session_id) FIRST (drop the HTTP session), then result_store.remove_session (drop the replay buffer), then lifecycle.close_session_with_reason. Both store ops are guarded by if-let-Some so stateless/partial configs are unaffected. Regression test seeds a session + a buffered response, cancels via the operator route, and asserts: lane closed, session_store.remove now returns false (already gone), result_store.session_count()==0, and append_response_if_session returns None. Green: core lib 574/0, fmt + clippy clean.

title: [QA100][P1][auth] Linearize credential revocation with lane admission
description:
Finding

Credential rotate/revoke cleanup is not linearized with in-flight authenticated requests. A request can validate bearer/session and capture DispatchContext, revocation then removes current sessions/lanes, and the request subsequently reaches resolve_lane and spawns a fresh Oracle lane under the now-revoked generation with pre-revocation scopes.

Evidence

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:5158-5266 authenticates/validates and later starts dispatch using captured context.
- close_http_principal_sessions at 6193-6213 removes session IDs, buffers and lanes in separate operations with no epoch/tombstone.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/lane.rs:763-830 resolves/spawns based only on session/principal strings and never rechecks credential/session generation.
- Interleaving after cleanup but before resolve creates a lane not present in the cleanup scan. Operator cancel has an analogous race.

Impact

After a revoke/rotate response becomes authoritative, previously accepted work can create new sessions/DB calls and retain grants beyond the intended immediate revocation point.

False-positive and duplicate checks

Already executing work needs a defined drain/cancel policy, but new lane admission after the revocation linearization point must not occur. This is distinct from OAuth client identity bead .56, credential persistence .90 and cancel invalidation .91. No tracker duplicate.

Proposed fix

Bind authenticated requests/lanes to credential and session epochs. Atomically bump/install a principal/session tombstone before cleanup; resolve_lane/send/execute must reject stale contexts. Then cancel/join in-flight work under documented semantics. Reconcile restart with persisted generation.

Guard tests

- Barrier every interleaving across auth, session validation, resolve, send, DB execute and revoke/rotate.
- After the linearization point no new dispatcher/DB call occurs; old grants fail and registries/buffers empty.
- In-flight outcome is explicitly completed/cancelled/in-doubt and audited.
- Restart agrees; unrelated principals continue.
- Model/property test proves no stale epoch lane exists.
close_reason: Linearize credential revocation with lane admission. qa112's LaneLifecycleState close-tokens only guard a resolution already IN FLIGHT when a close runs; a resolution that BEGINS after cleanup completes mints a fresh valid token, so a request that authenticated pre-revoke got a brand-new working lane. Fix: the credential store already exposes a monotonic per-principal generation (bumped by revoke/rotate under its file mutex, which linearizes auth-vs-revoke). Thread the generation observed at admission into DispatchContext; LaneLifecycleState keeps a per-principal principal_min_generation FLOOR. close_principal_sessions installs the floor (raise_min_generation, monotonic max) in the SAME lifecycle-lock critical section that invalidates close-tokens + drains the registry — the linearization point. resolve_lane checks the floor (a) at the top before consuming admission capacity (sequential fast-path refusal) and (b) again UNDER the lifecycle lock immediately before insert (airtight vs the concurrent-mint race: floor-before-insert => refused, or insert wins => concurrent drain removes it). Strict tightening: skipped when no floor OR context carries no generation for a floored principal is REFUSED fail-closed; OAuth/mTLS/anonymous use a different principal-key namespace so unaffected. Lock order Config->Lifecycle->Registry preserved (floor lives inside the existing lifecycle mutex, no new lock; credential-store file mutex is a leaf released before lane locks — no inversion). Per-principal floor (better than a global counter). 6 tests incl the primary proof stale_credential_generation_is_refused_after_revocation_completes (no factory build, no dispatch, no lane), concurrent-inflight compose-with-qa112, monotonic-under-reorder. Reviewed the linearization by hand. Green: core lib 608/0, fmt+clippy clean, downstream oraclemcp check passes. Worktree-agent co-authored.

title: [QA100][P1][self-update] Authenticate an immutable installer before executing it
description:
Finding

The self-update command executes the installer from the mutable main branch before authenticating any bytes. Supplying a pinned target version does not pin or authenticate the bootstrap code that gets shell execution.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp/src/main.rs:3699-3704 hard-codes raw GitHub URLs under main for both install.sh and install.ps1.
- Lines 3717-3739 pipe the downloaded Unix response directly into bash. Lines 3742-3767 download and invoke the PowerShell response.
- The requested version and verification posture are merely arguments passed to that already-running mutable script. Any archive checksum or signature verification happens later and cannot authenticate code that has already executed.
- Dry-run reports this as the same installer path, so pinned-version self-update remains branch-dependent.
- Full open, closed, and deferred tracker search found the original self-update feature and archive-verification work, but no immutable or authenticated bootstrap requirement.

Impact

A compromise, force update, CDN/cache substitution, or accidental regression on the repository main branch can execute arbitrary code in every self-update invocation, including invocations explicitly requesting a known release. This breaks reproducibility and moves the trust boundary ahead of the shipped checksum and signature controls.

False-positive and design-intent checks

A mutable one-line installer can be an intentional convenience for first install, but self-update with an explicit version claims a pinned release path. Verifying the eventual binary does not retroactively make previously executed installer code safe. The existing feature bead required resolution of latest, not mutable bootstrap trust.

Proposed fix

Resolve latest to a concrete release tag first. Fetch installer code from an immutable release tag or embed a minimal updater in the signed binary, and authenticate the installer bytes or release manifest before executing them. Keep Unix and Windows trust behavior identical and expose the resolved immutable source in dry-run output.

Guard tests

1. A self-update request for v0.8.0 never references a main-branch installer URL.
2. Latest resolves once to a concrete immutable tag and all later requests use that tag.
3. Tampered installer bytes are rejected before any child process executes.
4. Unix and Windows bootstrap verification are contract-equivalent.
5. Dry-run names the resolved tag, digest/signature evidence, target archive, and exact non-executed plan without secrets.
close_reason: Confirmed mutable-main bootstrap executed before archive verification. Replaced it with signed-binary embedded installer bytes, exact SHA-256 readback before spawn, one-time latest-to-concrete-tag resolution, direct argv execution, and cross-platform immutable-source dry-run evidence. Tests cover tampering, semver/tag rejection, mutable URL exclusion, explicit dry-run, and live latest resolution; focused tests and strict clippy pass.

title: [QA100][P1][http] Enforce absolute handshake, header, and body deadlines
description:
Finding

The native HTTP and HTTPS workers have only a per-read 30-second socket timeout. An unauthenticated peer can send a byte before each timeout and hold a scarce admitted worker forever.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/mod.rs:6037-6064 and 6118-6140 acquire the transport permit and spawn a thread before parsing or authenticating the request.
- Lines 6240-6266 set CONNECTION_IO_TIMEOUT as a socket read/write timeout.
- Lines 6272-6280 repeatedly call TLS complete_io until the handshake finishes without a monotonic total deadline.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/http/wire.rs:12-30 repeatedly reads until the header terminator, and lines 69-77 repeatedly read the declared body. Each successful trickle read resets the socket inactivity timeout.
- Admission bounds the number of threads but all regular permits can be occupied before request authentication and rate limiting.
- Full tracker search found transport capacity and soak work but no Slowloris or total parse/handshake deadline bead.

Impact

A small number of remote peers can exhaust all regular HTTP workers with trivial bandwidth and deny MCP, dashboard, and operator traffic indefinitely. HTTPS is exposed during the more expensive pre-authentication handshake as well.

False-positive and design-intent checks

The 30-second timeout bounds one blocked system call, not the entire handshake or request. Thread and byte caps prevent unbounded memory growth but do not preserve availability once every bounded slot is permanently occupied. Normal SSE response duration is a separate post-authenticated phase and need not share the request-ingress deadline.

Proposed fix

Apply monotonic absolute budgets separately to TLS handshake, complete headers, and declared body. Recompute remaining socket timeout before every read and reject when the total phase deadline expires; optionally add a minimum progress rate and bounded per-source pre-auth connection count. Preserve bounded shutdown and an authenticated control reserve.

Guard tests

1. A peer dripping one byte every half-timeout is closed within the absolute TLS, header, and body budget.
2. Saturating slow peers cannot prevent the documented authenticated control/health capacity.
3. A normal request at the byte limits succeeds within budget.
4. Cover plaintext and TLS, IPv4/IPv6, partial header, partial body, and stalled TLS handshake.
5. Shutdown joins all such workers within its documented bound.
close_reason: Confirmed unauthenticated Slowloris availability defect. Native TLS now advances handshake one bounded I/O step at a time under one monotonic absolute deadline (so rustls complete_io cannot reset it); headers and declared bodies each get independent absolute deadlines with remaining socket timeout recomputed before every read. Exact header/body caps remain accepted and cap-crossing terminators fail closed. Regression tests cover silent and valid byte-trickled TLS ClientHello peers, trickled plaintext headers/bodies, exact header/body boundaries, and over-cap headers. Focused 7/7, full oraclemcp-core 536/536, and strict all-target core clippy passed on the combined tree.

title: [QA100][P1][admission] Make operator and doctor reserve actually admissible
description:
Finding

Admission subtracts operator and doctor reserves from regular capacity, but there is no admission path that can consume those reserved slots. The advertised incident-response reserve is dead capacity.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/admission.rs:142-183 records global_cap, operator_reserved, and doctor_reserved but computes regular_global_cap by subtracting both.
- try_admit at lines 224-253 and the wrapper paths gate exclusively on regular_global_cap. subject_can_admit_locked at line 448 also compares against only that regular cap.
- Repository-wide search found no typed operator or doctor admission method and no path that admits up to global_cap.
- The N4 test at lines 637-676 proves only that regular callers cannot consume the reserve; it never proves that an authorized operator or doctor can.
- HTTP transport admission occurs before route/auth classification, using the generic path.
- Full tracker search found the closed reserve feature, not a defect bead for unreachable reserved capacity.

Impact

Under ordinary-agent saturation, operator cancellation, dashboard control, and doctor/readiness work cannot enter the supposedly reserved slots. Two host slots are withheld from everyone while the incident controls they were meant to protect still fail at capacity.

False-positive and design-intent checks

Keeping regular clients out of a reserve is correct, but a reserve without a server-derived privileged consumer cannot provide availability. Treating a caller-provided label as privileged would be unsafe; the missing piece must be authenticated and typed.

Proposed fix

Introduce a server-derived admission class with separate bounded accounting for Regular, Operator, and Doctor, and admit privileged classes only after authority is established. Solve the pre-auth transport boundary with a bounded handshake/control design rather than trusting request data. Make snapshots report actual class occupancy and keep total usage at or below global_cap.

Guard tests

1. Saturate all regular slots, then prove exactly the configured authorized operator and doctor work is admitted.
2. Unauthenticated and ordinary principals cannot claim either privileged class.
3. Total concurrent permits never exceeds global_cap and each class stays within policy.
4. Operator cancellation and health/readiness remain reachable under regular saturation at both transport and lane layers.
5. Permit release, drain, fair wait, and metrics remain correct for every class.
close_reason: Confirmed dead reserve and fixed end-to-end. Admission now accounts Regular/Operator/Doctor/control-probe permits separately, clamps reserves to the total ceiling, reports exact class occupancy/availability, and never exceeds global_cap. Ordinary API callers cannot select a privileged class. At transport saturation only one loopback, one-second pre-auth probe may enter; exact GET /healthz or /readyz promotes to Doctor, while /operator/v1 promotes only after server-derived operator authority succeeds. Forged headers, ordinary routes, remote pre-auth callers, extra probes, drain, and wrong-class promotions fail closed. Oracle lane and SSE controllers no longer subtract unreachable control reserves, so all measured data-plane capacity is usable; operator health and lane cancellation remain reachable while both lane and regular transport capacity are saturated. Regression evidence: focused admission 15/15, end-to-end reserve/security tests green, full oraclemcp-core lib 546/546, strict core all-target clippy green. Stronger hostile-remote availability requires pre-authenticated separate control ingress and is explicitly tracked by discovered-from bead .118.

title: [QA100][P1][connection] Bound post-authentication session setup round trips
description:
Finding

The thin connection applies session identity, canonical NLS statements, and operator-authored session setup before installing the configured Oracle call timeout. Those post-authentication round trips can hang outside the advertised per-call budget.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs:1005-1023 awaits Connection::connect, then apply_session_identity, canonical_nls_statements, and trusted session statements before storing opts.call_timeout.
- apply_session_identity at lines 1349-1412 delegates each call to execute_raw.
- execute_raw at lines 1414-1435 invokes the driver with default ExecuteOptions and no query timeout or outer wall-clock timeout.
- connect_timeout_seconds is threaded into Oracle Net transport connect options, but its documented scope is connection/authentication; it does not make these later arbitrary SQL/PLSQL setup calls honor call_timeout_seconds.
- README and configuration docs describe call_timeout_seconds as the per-round-trip Oracle ceiling and default it to 30 seconds.
- Full tracker search found connect-timeout pass-through and session-setup features, but not this gap between connect success and constructed connection state.

Impact

A login trigger aftermath, network blackhole, blocked package call, or trusted session statement can wedge startup, profile switching, pool replenishment, or doctor connection work indefinitely despite a finite configured call timeout. The half-created session also has no ordinary quarantine/cleanup owner yet.

False-positive and design-intent checks

Transport connect timeout and Oracle call timeout are distinct controls by design. Disabling call_timeout_seconds explicitly may permit an unbounded call, but the default finite value is currently ignored in this phase. Trusted statements are operator-authored, yet network and blocking behavior still require a bound.

Proposed fix

Apply the resolved call timeout to every post-connect setup round trip before the first one runs, using the same cancellation/uncertain-session handling as normal calls. Give the whole construction phase a bounded budget, close or discard the partial connection on any failure, and preserve structured phase context without logging SQL or secrets.

Guard tests

1. Each identity, NLS, and trusted-setup call observes the configured timeout.
2. A stalled setup call returns within budget and the partial session is closed/discarded.
3. Cancellation during setup cannot publish the connection into a pool or dispatcher.
4. Multiple setup statements share the documented total construction budget or have an explicitly tested per-call policy.
5. An explicit zero-timeout policy is documented and distinct from the default finite timeout.
close_reason: Verified the original finding was real when filed but is already fully resolved by commit 6e1656a (QA85). Current execute_raw constructs WireLimits with opts.call_timeout before every apply_session_identity, canonical NLS, and trusted session statement call, passes the resulting timeout_ms into the native driver, and the Cx absolute request deadline can only tighten it. The connection is not published until every setup call succeeds; any failure drops the half-built local driver connection. README's per-round-trip policy is therefore satisfied, while zero remains the explicit unbounded operator choice. Fresh source trace, git blame, QA85 full-workspace evidence, and current DB/core tests confirm no residual QA96 defect.

title: [QA100][P1][health] Propagate uncertain database failures instead of reporting privilege skips
description:
Finding

Oracle health probes turn every database error into an insufficient-privilege skip. Structurally uncertain connection failures are therefore swallowed before the dispatcher can quarantine the session.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/health.rs:237-257 makes detect_view_tier fall back from DBA to ALL on any error and returns None after any second error.
- Finding::skipped at lines 205-223 hard-codes the summary as insufficient privilege regardless of the supplied error.
- degrading_count_subcheck at lines 587-624 and the other health specializations convert actual diagnostic query errors into the same skip.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:6294 receives run_health as an ordinary successful report; it cannot inspect DbError::is_uncertain_session_state when errors were converted to findings.
- ORA disconnects, driver cancellation/timeouts, protocol failures, and SQL regressions are not evidence of missing view privilege.
- Full tracker search found privilege-degradation feature work but no bead distinguishing privilege errors from failed or uncertain sessions.

Impact

The tool can claim a benign privilege limitation during a broken or outcome-uncertain Oracle session, leave the connection unquarantined, and allow later work onto state whose transaction/protocol status is unknown. It also hides real health-query regressions and outages from operators.

False-positive and design-intent checks

Best-effort health aggregation is intentional and one ordinary check failure need not abort every independent check. That does not justify misclassifying all errors. The repository-wide fail-closed invariant explicitly requires structurally uncertain database state to quarantine rather than continue.

Proposed fix

Degrade from DBA to ALL only for recognized privilege or missing-view classes. Represent an ordinary non-uncertain per-check failure as status failed with truthful structured context. Immediately propagate structurally uncertain errors so the dispatcher applies its existing quarantine path. Keep raw SQL, credentials, and sensitive driver text redacted.

Guard tests

1. DBA ORA-01031 or ORA-00942 falls back to ALL.
2. A disconnect/cancel/timeout during the DBA probe does not try ALL and is propagated for quarantine.
3. A diagnostic SQL regression is failed, never labeled insufficient privilege.
4. One documented non-uncertain check failure can coexist with other completed checks.
5. Error rendering stays structured and secret-safe while preserving a stable Oracle error class/code.
close_reason: Verified and fixed uncertain health failure propagation with truthful failed/skip classification and guard tests

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Fresh verification confirmed the bug. Fixed health privilege degradation to fall back only on ORA-00942/ORA-01031; uncertain failures now propagate for dispatch quarantine; ordinary query regressions are secret-safe status=failed and independent checks continue. Updated preflight/doctor/live callers and dispatch output with checks_failed. Verified 26 focused health tests, strict DB clippy, UBS clean of critical/security findings, and oraclemcp lib suite 206/206 on the combined tree.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Pre-commit fresh-eyes compliance audit found and corrected three gaps before landing: cfg-gated live-xe callers still used the old run_health return type; Diagnostics Pack/Statspack preflight probes still swallowed uncertain failures; dispatcher quarantine/checks_failed lacked served regressions. Added uncertainty-aware feature probes, redacted doctor hard-failure coverage for late cancellation, ordinary-failed and quarantine/refusal dispatch tests, and fixed both live callers. Verified cargo check -p oraclemcp-db --features live-xe --test live_oracle plus 7 DB preflight, 4 doctor preflight, and 2 served dispatcher boundary tests.

title: [QA100][P1][schema-diff] Preserve exact Oracle identifier identity and reject ambiguous snapshots
description:
Finding

Schema diff uppercases every object name and silently collects duplicate keys into HashMaps. This collapses distinct quoted Oracle identifiers and can generate duplicate or mis-targeted migration steps; drop rendering also emits raw unquoted labels.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp-db/src/schema_diff.rs:25-31 defines identity as uppercased object type plus uppercased name.
- Oracle permits case-sensitive quoted identifiers that can coexist with ordinary folded identifiers, so unquoted FOO and quoted lowercase foo are different objects but become one key here.
- compare_schemas at lines 103-123 collects snapshots into HashMaps with last-value-wins semantics, while it later iterates every original object, so duplicate keys can both hide comparison state and emit repeated results.
- migration_plan at lines 198-205 formats object type and name directly into generated drop DDL without an Oracle identifier model or quoting validation.
- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/schema_diff_export.rs:141-179 checks only non-empty/control characters and size; it does not reject duplicate identities or validate allowed object types/identifiers.
- Independent review reached the same quoted-identifier and duplicate-key result; full tracker search found source-history quoted-name work but no schema-snapshot identity defect.

Impact

A migration preview can report the wrong changed object, omit a real change, produce repeated steps, or generate text targeting a different object than the snapshot intended. Because the operator flow exports reviewable/applyable migration artifacts, this is a high-risk correctness boundary even though execution remains separately gated.

False-positive and design-intent checks

Uppercasing is correct only for explicitly unquoted Oracle identifiers. The input model has no quoted/unquoted flag, owner, or canonical identity, so it cannot safely infer folding. Duplicate keys are malformed input rather than a meaningful schema state and should fail closed.

Proposed fix

Model owner, type, identifier text, and quotedness explicitly; canonicalize only unquoted identifiers and render every supported identifier with a central Oracle-safe formatter. Reject duplicate canonical identities within each snapshot before diffing. Restrict object types to a typed allowlist and keep unsupported or dependency-ambiguous operations review-only.

Guard tests

1. Ordinary FOO and quoted lowercase foo coexist and diff independently.
2. Escaped quotes, mixed-case owner/name, and unquoted folding round-trip exactly.
3. Identical and conflicting duplicate snapshot entries are rejected with indexed errors.
4. Malicious or malformed type/name text never becomes executable migration text.
5. Live Oracle fixtures prove generated identifiers target exactly the captured objects.
close_reason: Fresh verification confirmed lossy identity, duplicate last-write-wins, and raw DROP interpolation. Implemented explicit owner/name quote semantics, typed object allowlist, exact Oracle canonical identity, indexed duplicate rejection, safe qualified rendering, review-only dependency-ambiguous operations, operator contract/docs updates, focused and three-version live Oracle regression coverage.

title: [QA100][P2][mcp] Emit tools/list_changed whenever level changes visibility
description:
Finding

The server advertises tools.listChanged and filters tools/list by the current operating level, but a successful session level change does not enqueue notifications/tools/list_changed. Clients can retain a stale tool catalog until some unrelated refresh.

Evidence and reachability

- crates/oraclemcp-core/src/server.rs:488-519 builds tools/list from current surface state.
- descriptor_visible_for_surface at lines 1980-2000 changes visibility for execute, DDL, enable/disable, and destructive tools according to current_level and effective_ceiling.
- crates/oraclemcp/src/dispatch/mod.rs:5558-5620 processes oracle_set_session_level, mutates level/grants, audits an increase, and returns without enqueueing a notification.
- The only enqueue call in dispatch is at lines 5520-5521 after profile switch.
- served_capabilities_json advertises tools.listChanged true and its comment says a level/profile change should make the client re-fetch.
- The closed E6 bead acceptance explicitly required list_changed on operating-level change, but its close reason narrowed implementation to profile switch. Fresh source inspection proves the acceptance gap remains.
- No open tracker bead covers this regression.

Impact

After elevation, capable clients may not discover newly available write/DDL tools; after de-escalation or TTL expiry they may continue presenting tools that are no longer usable. Server-side enforcement remains intact, so this is protocol/ergonomics correctness rather than an authorization bypass.

False-positive and design-intent checks

Tool visibility genuinely changes on level transitions; this is not merely a metadata refresh. Enforcement correctly rejects stale calls, but MCP listChanged exists specifically to synchronize advertised catalogs. Profile-switch notification alone cannot cover TTL expiry or explicit set-level changes.

Proposed fix

Compare the visible descriptor fingerprint before and after every event that can change effective current level, including explicit elevation, de-escalation, TTL expiry, scope/ceiling transitions, and profile switch. Enqueue one list_changed notification only when the served catalog changes, using session/principal-aware delivery as required by the HTTP notification bead.

Guard tests

1. READ_ONLY to READ_WRITE emits exactly one notification and the refreshed list gains the expected tools.
2. De-escalation and TTL expiry emit and remove the tools.
3. A status/preview call or unchanged level emits nothing.
4. A profile switch whose visible fingerprint is unchanged emits nothing or follows the documented coalescing policy.
5. Stdio and stateful HTTP deliver the notification only to the affected MCP session.
close_reason: Fresh verification confirmed level-driven visibility changes were never signaled. The session-scoped catalog fingerprint now compares the exact served descriptors before/after valid requests, emitting one list_changed only when the catalog changes; this covers elevation, de-escalation, profile/custom-catalog changes, OAuth/ceiling effects, and lazy monotonic TTL expiry on the next request while preview/status/unchanged calls remain silent. Added a served regression proving READ_ONLY→READ_WRITE adds oracle_execute, drop/expiry remove it, and each real transition emits exactly once. Full core 615/615, oraclemcp 247/247, fmt and strict clippy green.

title: Serialize REF CURSOR and implicit result sets from thin results
description:
## Current gap

The current thin driver exposes enough public cursor APIs to consume REF CURSOR values now: `QueryValue::Cursor` includes a cursor id and column metadata, and blocking `fetch_rows` / `fetch_rows_with_columns` / `release_cursor` APIs exist. oraclemcp currently serializes generic `QueryValue::Cursor` as an unsupported placeholder in `crates/oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs`.

Agents therefore cannot inspect stored procedure outputs or PL/SQL blocks that return REF CURSORs, even when the driver has already received the cursor handle.

## Required change

Expand oraclemcp's thin result conversion so supported REF CURSOR values and implicit result sets are converted into bounded nested result objects.

## Safety requirements

- Enforce row, cell, byte, and nesting caps.
- Preserve child cursor column metadata.
- Release/close cursor ids deterministically, including error paths.
- Do not let child cursor fetching bypass the SQL guard; only consume cursors returned by already-authorized statements/procedures.
- Mark truncation explicitly when caps stop fetching.

## Acceptance criteria

- REF CURSOR cells no longer render as unsupported placeholders when the driver supplies a valid cursor id.
- Returned JSON includes child result columns, rows, row_count/fetched_count, and truncation metadata.
- Cursor release is tested on success and failure.
- Implicit result sets are surfaced if the driver's `QueryResult.implicit_resultsets` contains cursor values; unsupported shapes remain explicit.
- Unit tests cover zero-row cursor, multi-row cursor, caps, failure cleanup, and nested result serialization.
- A live 23ai/FREE test uses a small PL/SQL block or package procedure returning a REF CURSOR.
close_reason: Implemented bounded REF CURSOR and implicit-resultset serialization for thin results, including nested result JSON, row/cell/byte/depth caps, cursor expression and DBMS_SQL.RETURN_RESULT live 23ai coverage, failure-path cleanup coverage, full workspace gates, cargo deny, live feature tests, and single-bead compliance audit with closer evidence.

title: EPIC: oraclemcp + oracledb 0.7.3 cross-repo release
description:
EPIC — oraclemcp + oracledb 0.7.3 cross-repo release (lockstep +0.0.1, exact pin =0.7.3, NOT 1.0).

WHY: This is the "next big bump" even though it is only +0.0.1. It closes the round-2 field-test gaps against a real OCI environment, adds the full autonomously-testable python-oracledb differentiator set, ships the OMCP Operator Console dashboard, and lands a formal quality/test-hardening layer. Both repos ship in lockstep: the driver (oracledb) is developed, matrix-gated, and published FIRST; the server (oraclemcp) then bumps the exact =0.7.3 pin and builds on it. A third repo (plsql-intelligence) adopts the shared routine API afterward.

SCOPE (workstream epics under this root): A=driver hardening/OCI/differentiators/gate; B=server adopt/OCI/dashboard/doctor; C=autonomous secret-free OCI test harness; D6=quality and test-hardening; RELEASE=version-surface audit, release-ordering, local pre-tag gate, publish; K=accretive agent-UX additions; CONSUMER=plsql-mcp wiring.

NON-NEGOTIABLE INVARIANTS (carried from AGENTS.md + memory): (1) the fail-closed SQL guard may only ever be TIGHTENED, never loosened; every execute/recovery/apply path re-classifies at the point of action (SEC-1). (2) forbid(unsafe_code) in every published crate. (3) pure-Rust only, no C toolchain, no Instant Client, no bundled SQLite. (4) files-first persistence, append-only audit hash-chain, no database. (5) driver adapter confined to the single seam file oraclemcp-db/src/connection.rs. (6) no compat shims; migrate callers and delete old code. (7) CONFIDENTIALITY IS HARD: no live-OCI/customer identifier ever enters a committed artifact; all OCI tests use synthetic throwaway wallets/certs/tokens; scripts/secret_scan.sh (C4) enforces it.

CRITICAL PATH: C1 -> A2.1 -> A2.2 -> C2 -> A2.3 -> A5-gate -> publish-oracledb -> B1 -> B2.2 -> B2.4 -> b4-contract-tests -> publish-oraclemcp.

DAY-ONE RISK: A2.2 (cwallet.sso fallthrough) is the top-ranked item — it is the exact round-2 field failure. Sprint leads with the week-1 gates (A2.2 fallthrough coded+tested first; C4 secret-scan patterns; A5 matrix; D6.4 mutation gate proving the safety tests are real).

Source of truth for full detail: docs/plan/PLAN_0_7_3.md (v11). Each child bead is self-contained; you should not need the markdown.
notes: TESTING + LOGGING STANDARD (applies to every bead DoD): every unit test and e2e script emits STRUCTURED, DETAILED logs — inputs, the operation under test, expected-vs-actual, and the verdict — so a failing run is diagnosable from logs alone; e2e scripts log each step + timing. Prefer the STRONGEST oracle available (differential > metamorphic > round-trip > crash, per D6). No test asserts success silently; every fail-closed/negative path logs WHY it refused. Golden/cassette artifacts are secret-scanned (C4) before commit. New untrusted-decode surfaces carry a fuzz target; new concurrent paths carry a TSan run (D6.2).
close_reason: EPIC: oraclemcp+oracledb 0.7.3 cross-repo release — superseded by the shipped v0.8.0 (tag 2026-07-09); all workstreams closed. Reconciled during QA100 .10.

title: WORKSTREAM A: driver (oracledb 0.7.3)
description:
WORKSTREAM EPIC A — DRIVER (oracledb 0.7.3). repo: oracledb.

Goal: harden the pure-Rust thin driver, close the OCI wallet/IAM gap, land the full autonomously-testable python-oracledb differentiator set, and matrix-gate + publish the driver FIRST (server pins it exactly).

Contains: A1 stability/hardening (GH#14 timeouts is the marquee bug), A2 OCI wallet (legacy-3DES decrypt + cwallet.sso fallthrough), A3 IAM TokenSource seam, A4 differentiators (Tier-1 offline + Tier-2 live lane), A5 matrix gate + OCI lane, A6 VERSION const, A7 pyshim edges, A8 enable pipelining, publish-oracledb-073.

Gate: a green full version-matrix artifact (results-<sha>.json) committed for the release SHA before the tag. The driver is 1.0.0-rc.1/API-frozen; we deliberately do NOT cut 1.0 this cycle.
close_reason: Workstream complete — all leaf beads closed; driver 0.8.2 + server 0.8.0 published.

title: A1.1: GH#14 connect/idle/keepalive timeouts (marquee bug)
description:
A1.1 — GH#14 connect/idle/keepalive timeouts (the marquee driver bug). repo: oracledb. Blocks A5 + B1.

WHY: the driver connect ignores the parsed EXPIRE_TIME (keepalive) and TRANSPORT/TCP_CONNECT_TIMEOUT, and has no read-inactivity deadline, so half-open/idle connections hang FOREVER. Reported downstream in the round-2 stack. This is the release's marquee bug.

CHANGE (cited): today lib.rs:9390 has a fixed 20s timeout and the framing reads at lib.rs:9247/:9264 use read_exact with NO deadline. Add ConnectOptions fields connect_timeout: Option<Duration> and inactivity_timeout: Option<Duration>; default inactivity_timeout operator-set (propose 300s); derive the keepalive interval from EXPIRE_TIME; apply the timeout to CONNECT + ACCEPT + ALL post-auth data reads. Enumerate EVERY read_exact site in the framing layer, not just :9247/:9264.

TEST/DoD: with inactivity_timeout=5s, EXPIRE_TIME=30s, server silent -> the 5s deadline fires (NOT a 30s hang); a keepalive success RESETS the deadline. Deterministic via a controllable mock transport.
acceptance_criteria: AC1: with inactivity_timeout set small + a silent/half-open server, the read fails at ~the deadline (Error::CallTimeout), NOT a forever-hang (the GH#14 fix). AC2: TCP SO_KEEPALIVE enabled from EXPIRE_TIME (Description.expire_time) matching python-oracledb, so dead peers are detected at the TCP layer.
